Tafseer:
Commentary of Verses 1–2: "Alif, Lam, Ra. These are the verses of the clear Book. Indeed, We have sent it down as an Arabic Quran that you might understand."

The Quranic Name of the Surah: "Alif, Lam, Ra" is the Quranic name of this Surah. This same name is also given to Surah Yunus and Surah Hud. The primary purpose of a name is naming, hence there is no need to dig deep into their meanings. It is sufficient to remember that shared names indicate a sharing of themes and subject matters. Thus, as we have indicated in the commentary of the previous two Surahs, this aspect is present in all these Surahs. The core subject of discussion in them is one, though the style of discussion and argumentation is distinct in each.

The Concept of "Clear Book" (Kitab-i-Mubeen): "These are the verses of the clear Book." A clear Book is one that is entirely plain and distinct in its expression and argumentation, whose every point is proven by undeniable arguments, and whose style of discussion and vision is heart-touching, reassuring, and dispeller of all confusions. The meaning is that there is no need for any external miracle or sign to witness the truth of this Book, as the deniers are demanding; rather, the arguments for its truth and veracity, which are as bright as the sun, are present within it itself, provided people listen to it with open ears and ponder over its arguments.

A Great Favor upon the Arabs: "Indeed, We have sent it down as an Arabic Quran that you might understand." The address is to the Arab people in general and to the Quraish in particular: a great favor of Allah has been bestowed upon you, in that this greatest blessing of Allah has been revealed in your Arabic language so that you may understand it, value it, convey it to others, and explain it to them. This is one aspect of this Book being a clear Book, and it also contains a threat for the Quraish: if you do not value this blessing, there will be no one more wretched than you. The greater the blessing, the greater the reward you will deserve if you value it.

The Meaning of "Ahsan-al-Qasas": In "Ahsan-al-Qasas" (the best of stories), "qasas" is used in the sense of a story and narration. It is not used in the sense of the verbal noun (masdar), i.e., the act of narrating a story. If it were in the sense of the verbal noun, then according to the well-known usage of the language, the definite article (alif-lam) would not have come upon it; rather, it would have been "Ahsanu qasasin". For this reason, in our view, its meaning is: We relate to you the best story. The interpretation that "We relate it to you in the best style" is not correct. The parallels of the Quran support our view. For example, in Surah Al-A'raf: "So relate the stories (al-qasas) that perhaps they will give thought." In Surah Al-Qasas: "And when he came to him and related to him the stories (al-qasas)." In our view, "Ahsan-al-Qasas" is a composition of speech similar to the parallel in the Quran: "Allah has sent down the best statement (Ahsan-al-Hadeeth)". It is also worthy of attention here that by taking "Ahsan-al-Qasas" in the sense of the verbal noun, the object (maf'ool) disappears, whereas the verb demands its object, and in the Quran, it has appeared with its object everywhere.

These are a few indications regarding the composition of speech. As for the question of why this narration of Joseph (peace be upon him) was described as "Ahsan-al-Qasas", we have clarified some of its aspects in our introductory discussions, and we will, God willing, point to some others at their respective places ahead.

A Narration Serving as a Mirror for the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): In the verses above, the address is general and its nature, as we have indicated, was to warn the Quraish. Now, from this verse, the direction of speech turns directly towards the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). Addressing you, it is said: We are relating to you an excellent story, and this story is one of the countless blessings of this Quran which We are revealing to you, before which you were entirely unaware of it.

If you ponder here, it will be known that two highly important aspects of this story have been made clear to you. One is that it is "Ahsan-al-Qasas", and the second is that it is an extremely clear proof of your messengership. As far as its being the best of stories is concerned, we have clarified it above. If a narration is true in itself, and for the one to whom it is being related, it also becomes like a mirror in which he can see all the ups and downs of his life up to the final stages of success, then there can be no narration more lesson-giving, blessed, and valuable than that narration. For the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), the nature of the story of Joseph was exactly this. In it, the complete map of your present and future was shown to you, in which some places were very harsh but the final destination was highly glorious. Although the Cave of Thawr also came in this path, from the darknesses of the Cave of Thawr, the rule of Madinah was also visible, and within the kind life of Mecca, the glimpse of that day was also prominent when the rebels of Mecca would bow their knees and beg you for mercy and kindness.

The Nature of the Narration being a Proof of Messengership: It is a proof of messengership because the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was unlettered (ummi). You were neither aware of this story, nor did you possess any means to become aware of it. It was the grace of the Quran that you became aware of it, and became aware of it with such correctness, truth, latitude, and detail that even the People of the Book were not aware of it. From this perspective, this was a proof of your messengership for your nation as well as for the People of the Book—that if you are not honored with divine revelation, how did you come to know these things with such thoroughness, correctness, and truth! By the word "min qablihi" (before it), the objective is to draw attention to the actual fact that if before this Quran you had even the slightest acquaintance with this story, then in the long period of forty years, it would have surely come upon your tongue at some point. Then why do your opponents not think that if this is not the effusion of divine revelation, where did this spring suddenly burst forth? It should also be kept in mind here that this story is indeed present in the Torah and the Talmud, but firstly, as we have submitted, you did not possess any means to become aware of them; then, at every step, there is a difference between their description and the description of the Quran, and whoever ponders over this difference with justice will reach the same conclusion: that the description of the Quran is entirely natural, while the description of the Torah is entirely contrary to reason, nature, and incompatible with the dignity of prophethood.

[Remember] when Joseph said to his father, "O my father, indeed I have seen eleven stars and the sun and the moon; I saw them prostrating to me."

The Dream of Joseph: Now the introduction to the actual narration begins. Joseph related a dream of his to his father Jacob (peace be upon them): eleven stars and the sun and the moon are prostrating before me. Joseph bringing this dream first of all to the knowledge of his father is a proof of his extraordinary soundness of nature, good intention, and auspiciousness. If a person of a shallow nature had seen such a dream, he would have first of all beaten the drum of his greatness among others; but Joseph was highly dignified, composed, and serious from his very childhood. When he saw this dream, he felt that this dream was not of the nature of a muddled dream, but rather a dream of importance, which is why he presented it first of all before his father, in whom he had the greatest trust from every aspect. If you ponder over the words of the verse, it will be known that the humility present in his nature is also prominent in the style of presenting the dream. Upon going before Jacob, he did not bluntly say that: I saw eleven stars and the sun and the moon prostrating before me; rather, speaking the first part of the dream—"indeed I have seen eleven stars and the sun and the moon"—he paused, because in the subsequent point his greatness was prominent, the expression of which his humble nature hesitated to make; but since expression was necessary, after a brief pause, he said: "I saw them prostrating to me." Those who possess a taste for Arabic can estimate that in the repetition of the verb "ra'aytu" (I saw), there is this eloquence: the hesitation that Joseph experienced while relating the dream, especially while relating the event of prostration, is manifest.

He said, "O my son, do not relate your vision to your brothers lest they plot against you a plot. Indeed, Satan to man is a clear enemy."

Jacob's Interpretation and His Instruction to Joseph: As soon as Jacob heard the dream, he estimated that this was an indication of being elevated to the rank of prophethood, but before this glad tidings, he strictly forbade Joseph: do not relate this dream before your brothers, lest they burn with jealousy and engage in some plot against you. This matter should remain clear here: Joseph had eleven brothers in total, ten of whom were from his stepmothers. Only the youngest brother, Benjamin, was from his own mother. Here, the allusion is towards those ten stepbrothers. Since the hostility of these brothers towards Joseph was clear, Jacob feared that if they heard Joseph's dream, the fire of their jealousy would burn further, and it would not be strange if, becoming blind with jealousy, they made some dangerous plot to cause him harm. "Indeed, Satan to man is a clear enemy"—that is, the open enemy Satan is indeed present; let him not put them on some path of mischief.

And thus will your Lord choose you and teach you of the interpretation of narratives and complete His favor upon you and upon the family of Jacob, as He completed it upon your fathers before, Abraham and Isaac. Indeed, your Lord is Knowing and Wise.

When Jacob heard the dream, since this matter was in his knowledge—that prophethood initiates with true dreams alone, and the very appearance of this dream was telling that this was a true dream and was predicting a glorious future for the dreamer—for this reason, he said: what you have seen is correct. Soon that time is coming when your Lord will choose you for the rank of prophethood. In "and teach you of the interpretation of narratives" (wa yu'allimuka min ta'weeli-al-ahadeeth), there is an allusion to two things. One is to the point that Allah Almighty Himself will make the reality of this dream clear to you. Accordingly, when Joseph saw its reality with his own eyes, he said: "'O my father, this is the explanation of my vision of before; my Lord has made it reality. And He was certainly good to me when He took me out of prison and brought you [here] from bedouin life after Satan had induced [estrangement] between me and my brothers. Indeed, my Lord is Subtle in what He wills. Indeed, He is the Knowing, the Wise. My Lord, You have given me [something] of sovereignty and taught me of the interpretation of dreams...': O my father, this is the interpretation of my dream which I saw before; my Lord has made it a reality and He has been highly kind to me when He took me out of the prison and brought you people from the village, after Satan had put corruption between me and my brothers; my Lord accomplishes what He wills with extreme beauty; He is highly Knowing and Wise. O my Lord, You have indeed granted me sovereignty and also taught me the interpretation of dreams."

The Knowledge of the Interpretation of Dreams: The second is that since a dream is one of the mediums of the sciences of prophethood, and in dreams, realities manifest in the form of metaphors which demand a special mental affinity to understand, for this reason, Allah Almighty also grants the Prophets (peace be upon them) a special taste and a special knowledge of the interpretation of dreams. When Jacob felt that within this dream lay the glad tidings of prophethood for Joseph, at the same time, this matter also became clear to him: now Joseph will also be granted the knowledge of the interpretation of dreams so that he can correctly understand the realities that befall him in the form of dreams and can comply with the divine intent. Accordingly, ahead in this Surah, it will come that Allah Almighty raised his rank in this knowledge to such a degree that eventually this very knowledge became the medium for his acquisition of the kingdom of Egypt.

The Root of Blessing is Religion and Shariah: "and complete His favor upon you..." By "favor" (ni'mah) is meant the favor of religion and Shariah. The being of other worldly things as a blessing is a relative matter. Only when a servant receives the blessing of religion and Shariah is the favor completed. Jacob said: just as Allah Almighty blessed your ancestors Abraham and Isaac with His favor, similarly He will bless you and the family of Jacob with this favor, and the dream you have seen is the glad tidings of this completion of favor. Allah is highly Knowing and Wise. He possesses the knowledge of everything, and whatever He does is based on wisdom.

The Subject Ahead. Verses 7 to 22:

The Narration of Joseph (peace be upon him): After the introduction above, now begins the actual narration of Joseph and his brothers. Since the primary objective in relating this narration was not storytelling, but rather to answer those many questions which were arising in that era within the minds of both the near and far regarding the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his call, for this reason, warning was given at the very beginning: "Certainly there were in Joseph and his brothers signs for those who ask." That is, those who are in confusion as to what will happen ahead and how it will happen will find the answers to their such questions in this narration. Then, at the end of the section, it is said: "And Allah is predominant over His affair, but most of the people do not know." Recite the verses ahead in this light.

"Certainly there were in Joseph and his brothers signs for those who ask."

A Warning before the Narration: This verse, as we indicated in the introduction, is a warning before initiating the actual narration—that the addressees should not listen to it merely as a story, but rather within it are implicit the answers to those many questions which are emerging in the minds of both the opponents and supporters in this era of the Islamic call. In the previous two Surahs of this group, the theme of the victory of truth and the defeat of falsehood has been described in various styles, but at the time when this theme was described, the darkness of disbelief was spread over the entire country in such a way that it was not easy to conceive that this darkness would one day completely vanish; and that the person who today, with his few highly oppressed companions, is the target of all kinds of miseries and oppressions at the hands of the rebels of the time, a day would come when all these rebels would bow their knees before him and beg him for mercy and kindness. The Quran related this narration to bring this concept close to the minds so that all questions and doubts present or arising in the future regarding this matter in the minds are removed, and people estimate how the glories of God manifest and how His intentions and plans are brought to work.

When they said, "Joseph and his brother are more beloved to our father than we, while we are a clan. Indeed, our father is in clear error."

The Anger of Joseph's Brothers: "Usbah" means a group, gang, and assembly, specifically that group in which the prejudice of blood is also present. In the nomadic period of life, when organized governments did not exist, reliance for support and defense was entirely on the prejudice of family and tribe. Considered the most honorable and influential was that family within which the young men rising for support and defense were the most numerous. This family would acquire the leadership of the nation and tribe, and would rule. The brothers of Joseph, keeping this very matter in view, expressed anger among themselves at their father's lack of wisdom and foresight (God forbid): that the status of gang and group belongs to us, the medium of support and defense will be formed by us, and awe over others will be established on our strength; but our father's state is such that he has love for Joseph and his brother Benjamin; what can be a greater mistake and error than this.

"Kill Joseph or cast him out to [another] land; the face of your father will be free for you, and you will be after that a righteous people."

The Literal Meaning of "Salih": The word "salih" (righteous/prosperous) here is exactly in its literal sense. In Arabic, if they say "saluhat haalu fulaanin", its meaning is: his state became completely fine, his anxiety vanished, and he got delivered from the thorn that was pricking him.

The Consultation of Joseph's Brothers: This is that cure which Joseph's brothers thought of to remove their anxiety. They made this plan: either kill Joseph or throw him away to some distant place so that their father's entire attention turns towards them and this thorn which is pricking them is extracted.

Some people have interpreted "and you will be after that a righteous people" to mean becoming good after performing this crime, but this meaning cannot be of this sentence in any way. Leaving other aspects aside, the grammatical structure of the sentence itself rejects it; obviously, its conjunction is on the previous response to the command, because of which, whatever rule applies to that will apply to this. From this consultation of Joseph's brothers, it becomes entirely clear that their primary grudge was against Joseph alone, and in this grudge, the primary factor was not merely their being stepbrothers, but rather his high capabilities which had begun to emerge in him from that very age, and seeing which Jacob loved him extraordinarily. If the grudge had been merely due to being stepbrothers, then Benjamin was also a stepbrother; after all, why did they not make any plan to put him away!

A speaker among them said, "Do not kill Joseph but throw him into the bottom of the well; some caravan will pick him up, if you would be doing [something]."

"Ghayabah" refers to the bottom of the well, and also to a crude, unbricked well. On desert routes through which caravans pass, such wells existed which in normal conditions lay just like that, but when a caravan passed, they became lively.

One of the ten brothers, it appears, had some soft corner in his heart for Joseph. He advised: do not kill him; if you must do something, do this: throw him into some well on the route of the caravans. Some caravan passing by will extract him. Since in that era, slave-trading was a common custom, it is possible this thought also occurred: the people of the caravan will either make him their slave or take him to some city and sell him. In this way, his life will also be saved and the thorn of your side will also be extracted. Eventually, everyone agreed on this advice.

11–12. They said, "O our father, what is [the matter] with you that you do not trust us with Joseph, while indeed, we are to him sincere counselors? Send him with us tomorrow that he may eat well and play. And indeed, we will be his guardians."

The Deceit to Jacob: "Yarta' wa yal'ab" literally means eating well and playing, but this is an extremely beautiful expression for having a picnic. In the nomadic period of life, among the ways of entertaining oneself that have been highly popular and beloved, picnics have held a special importance. The poets of the Jahiliyyah mention it with great interest in their odes. The brothers of Joseph also, after agreeing on the aforementioned opinion, tried to show this very green garden to Jacob to win him over. All together, they came to his presence. First, to establish their trust, they said: what is the matter that you do not trust us in the matter of Joseph, whereas we are his great well-wishers and lovers. Then, they presented their picnic program before him: tomorrow we have made a program of recreation, and our heartfelt desire is that Joseph also participate in this program, he will eat some fruits, play with us, and you rest completely assured that there is no apprehension of any matter; if any danger occurs, we are all present for his protection.

13–14. He said, "Indeed, it saddens me that you should take him, and I fear that a wolf would eat him while you are of him unmindful." They said, "If a wolf should eat him while we are a clan, indeed we would then be losers."

Jacob said: if I have grief and apprehension, it is of the point that you take him and you engage in your play and your interests, and in your negligence, some wolf eats him. It appears that in that area, wolves were numerous and incidents of their attacks on humans used to happen. Hearing this apprehension of Jacob (peace be upon him), they replied with great pride: in the presence of our entire party, if a wolf eats him, who can be more wretched and unsuccessful than us. It appears that after this reassurance of theirs, Jacob, even if reluctantly, allowed Joseph to go with them, and the first stage of their conspiracy was successfully completed.

15–17. So when they took him and agreed to put him into the bottom of the well... And We inspired to him, "You will surely inform them of this affair of theirs while they do not perceive." And they came to their father at night, weeping. They said, "O our father, indeed we went racing and left Joseph with our possessions, and a wolf ate him. But you would not believe us, even if we were truthful."

In these verses, it should be kept in mind that the response to "So when" (falamma) is: "They said, 'O our father...'". To compress the speech, several intermediate matters have been placed under "So when" itself. That is: when they decided to throw him into the well, and Joseph was also inspired with this glad tidings—that you will be delivered from this calamity and a day will come when you will inform them of this performance of theirs—and they came weeping to their father at night, then they said: we left Joseph with the possessions and went racing far away, and a wolf ate Joseph. The benefit of this compression was that reference was made to all parts of the narration and the attention of the addressee was not allowed to deviate from the original point.

Now cast a glance at its parts:

"So when they took him and agreed to put him into the bottom of the well..."—that is, they somehow pleased Jacob and took Joseph with them, and agreed on the point of putting him into the well. From this, it emerges that although the difference of opinion remained till the end as to whether they should kill Joseph or throw him into the well, eventually they all agreed and acted upon the proposal of the well.

The Glad Tidings to Joseph: "And We inspired to him, 'You will surely inform them of this affair of theirs while they do not perceive.'" Here, by "inspiration" (wahy) is not meant the technical, prophetic revelation, but rather putting the point into the heart. When a trial befalls the righteous at the hands of the unjust and the wicked, at the very time when Allah gives leeway to the unjust, He also sends down tranquility, peace, consolation, and glad tidings upon the heart of the oppressed from the Unseen. Its experience is had more or less by every person who is blessed with raising any trouble in the path of Allah. This thing descends upon the heart on its own, and then colors the heart in its own color in such a way that the importance of even the greatest calamity does not remain equal to a straw of grass. For Joseph (peace be upon him), this point was put into his heart from Allah: this trial is temporary and transitory. That time will come when you will inform these people of their performance, and you will be at such a high position at that time that they will not even be able to assume that this is their same brother talking to them whom they had thrown into the dark well. Its details will come under verse 90.

The Word-Weaving of the Brothers: "And they came to their father at night, weeping." That is, performing this deed, they came late in the night to their father pulling faces and weeping. In coming late in the night, it is possible this interest was kept in view: if any thought of search and investigation arose in their father's heart, no possibility of it should remain.

"They said, 'O our father, indeed we went racing and left Joseph with our possessions, and a wolf ate him. But you would not believe us, even if we were truthful.'" This is the original point which they presented in their defense. "Istibaq" means running, rushing, and exceeding one's companion. Ahead in this Surah, "astabaqa-al-baab" has appeared, i.e., they both rushed to the door. These people did not show much intelligence in finding an excuse for themselves. The apprehension of the wolf which Jacob had expressed before them, they made up their point from that: since the apprehension of the wolf is already present in their father's mind, why not take refuge under its cover. Accordingly, they repeated Jacob's own point: we left Joseph with the possessions and went racing far away, and then a wolf came and ate Joseph. Speaking this point, they indeed spoke it, but they themselves were not reassured that Jacob would believe their word. Accordingly, the thief of their heart also came upon their tongue, and at the same time they also spoke: "But you would not believe us, even if we were truthful."

And they brought upon his shirt false blood. [Jacob] said, "Rather, your souls have enticed you to something, so patience is most fitting. And Allah is the one sought for help against that which you describe."

"Sawwalat lahu nafsuhu kaza": his soul decorated and made it easy and light in his eyes; Satan put such a point in his eyes and made it easy. 

The Meaning of "Sabrun Jameel" (most fitting patience): "Sabrun Jameel" can be the subject (mubtada), because the capability to be a subject arises in a described indefinite noun, and it can also be the predicate of an omitted subject. We have translated considering it the subject, and have opened the omitted predicate in the translation. In such situations, the reason for the predicate being omitted is that it is completely clear, and its benefit is that the entire attention remains focused on the original point. By "sabrun jameel" is meant that patience which is clean of all kinds of wailing, complaining, and mourning. If the difference between the Quran and the Torah is to be seen, see only this one place in the Torah. The Quran praises his beautiful patience, and in the Torah, it says: "And Jacob rent his clothes, and put sackcloth upon his loins, and mourned for his son many days." (Genesis, 37: 34).

To make their point believed by Jacob, these people did this device: they also brought false bloodstains of some animal on Joseph's shirt; but Jacob did not believe their word even for a single moment. As soon as he heard, he said: this is all your fabricated story; so may the support of beautiful patience be granted, and against what you describe, Allah alone is the One whose help is sought so that its knot is opened.

And there came a caravan, and they sent their water-drawer, and he let down his bucket. He said, "Good news! This is a boy." And they concealed him as merchandise - and Allah was Knowing of what they did.

The Meaning of "Warid": "Warid" literally means one who descends upon a watering place or spring. In Arabia, caravan-people did thus: at whichever well they had to stop, they sent one of their men slightly beforehand so that he would arrange for water etc. Here, by "warid" is meant that.

The Arrangements of the Sustaining God: The brothers of Joseph, throwing him into the well, repaired to their home. On this side, the Generous and Sustaining Lord made this arrangement: a caravan came by and sent its water-arranger to the well. He let down the bucket, and saw that there was a boy in the well. He cried out with joy: good news, there is a boy in this! In that era, slave-trading was a common custom. They thought: let us go, a profitable thing has been found; we will sell him somewhere; and with this thought—lest some claimant arise to the right and left—they tried to keep this event a secret, they did not let it be publicized. "and Allah was Knowing of what they did"—and Allah knew well what they were doing. They were completing their plan, and Allah was completing His plan. They had found a slave; they were happy that selling him they would acquire some money, and Allah willed that this slavery of Joseph should prove to be the preface to the kingdom of Egypt.

And they sold him for a reduced price - a few dirhams - and they were, concerning him, of those content with little.

The Meaning of "Bakhs" and "Zuhd": "Shara yashri" comes in both meanings of buying and selling. Here it has come in its second meaning. "Bakhs" means deficient and contemptible. "Zahada fish-shay'i": he became indifferent to it and left it. The ascetic (zahid) of the world is called a zahid because he becomes indifferent and independent of the world and the resources of the world.

The Sale of Joseph (peace be upon him): The meaning is that these people, as soon as they reached Egypt, sold him for an extremely contemptible price, in exchange for a few counted dirhams; they had not spent any money to acquire Joseph, such that they would care to recover their cost and make some further profit on it. A thing had come into hand free of cost; at whichever price it was sold, for them it was profit upon profit. Accordingly, they sold him probably to the very first buyer, at whichever price came out of his mouth. What did they know that the boy they were selling was the light of the house of Jacob and the Prophet of God, and very soon the entire kingdom of Egypt was to come under his thumb; when they were not aware of any of these things, they had to be careless.

And the one who bought him from Egypt said to his wife, "Make his stay honorable. Perhaps he will benefit us, or we will adopt him as a son." And thus We established Joseph in the land that We might teach him of the interpretation of narratives. And Allah is predominant over His affair, but most of the people do not know.

Clarification of a Grammatical Difficulty: In this verse lies a grammatical difficulty—namely, the conjoined (ma'toof-alayh) of "walinu'allimahu" (and that We might teach him) is not present. Examples of this style have passed behind too and we have clarified that sometimes the conjoined is omitted due to extreme clarity, it is understood from the context, and if the occasion demands, the entire statement can be assumed omitted there which can fill the existing gap. Here, in our view, before "walinu'allimahu", "linajtabiyahu" (that We might choose him) is omitted, and the conjunction "wa" (and) is pointing towards it; i.e., the entire statement is: in this way, We prepared the ground in the land for Joseph so that We might choose him for Our work, and so that We might teach him the interpretation of things.

A New Turn in the Life of Joseph (peace be upon him): Now from here, the life of Joseph (peace be upon him) takes a new turn. The one who bought him from the people of Egypt, it is known from the Torah, was an honorable official of the government, probably the chief officer of the royal bodyguard and an extremely noble man. From the Torah, it is also known that as soon as he saw Joseph (peace be upon him), he perceived that this was the light of some highly noble family whom these sellers had caught from somewhere. Accordingly, he directed his wife: do not keep him like a slave, but rather with hospitality; it is highly possible he will cause us benefit, or we will adopt him as a son. It appears that until that time, he did not have any children, which is why he thought: if we do not have any children, we will adopt him. In this way, he made Joseph the master of his entire house, as is clear from the Torah.

Joseph's (peace be upon him) Selection for Prophethood: "And thus We established Joseph"—that is, in this way, We prepared the ground in Egypt for Joseph so that We might select him for that work of prophethood for which We wanted to select him, and teach him that interpretation of things and dreams which would eventually, as details will follow, practically prepare the ground for him for the kingdom of Egypt.

Warning to the Quraish: "And Allah is predominant over His affair, but most of the people do not know." All the narration described above is its summary—that the intentions and plans of God are brought to work in this way, but those whose gaze is only on the apparent do not know. It should be kept in mind here that this entire narration is being related to the Quraish, and in this final sentence, the veil is removed from their eyes—that if they want, they may see their future too in this narration, and also the future of that Prophet against whom they are engaged day and night in various kinds of conspiracies.

And when he to full strength attained, We gave him judgment and knowledge. And thus We reward the doers of good.

Allah's Reward upon Joseph: This is that reward which Allah Almighty bestowed upon Joseph later, and to which reference is made in the verse above. It is said: when he reached maturity of age, We blessed him with judgment and knowledge, and We reward the doers of good in this way. The word "ashudd" (full strength) comes for youth and also for mature age, as for example: "until, when he reaches maturity and reaches forty years". "Judgment" (hukm) and "knowledge" ('ilm) here are expressions of prophethood. By "judgment" is meant the power of decision and also rule, and both of these things were granted to Joseph. By "knowledge" is meant that knowledge which is granted to the Prophets through revelation. At the end, it was made clear that this reward which occurred upon Joseph, occurred because he was a doer of good. He recognized the rights and duties of God and stood complete in every trial, so God selected him for His prophethood.

The Subject Ahead. Verses 23 to 34: The Second Stage of Joseph's (peace be upon him) Trial: As soon as the first stage of trial ended, the second stage initiated. The first stage was of his brothers' jealousy, hatred, and enmity, as a result of which he was thrown into a dark well. This second stage manifested in the form of love and passion from the wife of the Al-Aziz, as a result of which Joseph was put in prison without crime. But just as he was successful in the first trial, similarly Allah Almighty granted him highly glorious success in this second trial too. Recite the verses.

"And she, in whose house he was, seduced him, and she closed the doors and said, "Come, you!" He said, "[I seek] refuge in Allah. Indeed, he is my master; he has made my stay honorable. Indeed, the wrongdoers will not succeed."

The Meaning of "Murawadah": "Rawadat-hu 'an nafsihi" means she tried to incline him towards adultery through deceit and trickery.

The Meaning of "Hayta laka": "Hayta laka" means "halumma laka" and "ta'al", i.e., come.

The Meaning of the Word "Rabb": "Innahu rabbi"—the word "rabb" (lord/master) here is in its general literal sense of master and owner. Ahead in verses 41–42, it has also been used in this sense. For example: "As for one of you, he will give his master wine to drink." "Mention me before your master."

The Trap of Lust for Joseph: We have mentioned above that from here, the new era of Joseph's life begins. Until now, he was the victim of hatred and jealousy; when he got delivered from that, passion and lust tried to throw their traps upon him, and this trial proved to be far more severe than the first trial. The wife of the Egyptian who had bought him fell in love with him, and the matter reached the point that one day she closed her doors and said: come now. Joseph, referencing her husband, said: he is my master and he has kept me in an extremely good way. This would be a great faithlessness and ingratitude if I performed any such action with his wife. In this case, I will be a wrongdoer, and the wrongdoers will never succeed. If you ponder over this sentence of Joseph, it will be known that within it lies great psychological eloquence. In front of a woman who was blind with emotions and careless of God and the Hereafter, preaching about God and the Hereafter was, obviously, equivalent to playing a lute before a buffalo. If her excitement could be cooled down slightly, it could be cooled down only by this sentence which Joseph spoke. If there had been even a trace of nobility in her, she must have thought: here is this youth, who is so influenced by the ordinary kindness of his master, and has such care and regard for his mastery, that despite my open invitation, he considers performing any faithlessness with him as the destruction of both his world and Hereafter; and here am I, who am his wife, have given myself to him, have made him the master of my chastity, am sitting as the queen of his house, am disposing of his wealth like an owner, but my loyalty with him is such that I am inviting his bought slave to love, being blind with lust.

The Referent of the Pronoun in "Innahu rabbi": Some people have considered the referent of the pronoun in "innahu rabbi" (indeed he is my lord) to be God, but this is mere affectation. The context rejects it. In Arabic, compounds like "rabb-al-maal" (owner of wealth), "rabb-al-bayt" (master of the house), and "rabb-ad-daar" (master of the mansion) are present, and above you saw that Joseph himself used the word "rabb" for his master. How many words are there in the language which are used for God as well as for His servants, but in both forms their meanings are entirely distinct.

And she certainly determined [to seduce] him, and he would have inclined to her had he not seen the proof of his Lord. Thus [it was] that We might avert from him evil and immorality. Indeed, he was of Our chosen servants.

The Meaning of "Proof of Lord" (Burhan-i-Rabb): By "proof of Lord" is meant that divine light which Allah Almighty deposits within the nature of every human being. Which is the medium of distinction between good and evil, and which also incites to good and prevents from evil. Allah Almighty bestows this light indeed upon everyone, but the divine sunnah is that those who value it and accept its guidance, within them it becomes stronger and stronger, until it becomes so strong that even on occasions of severe trials, it protects man from falling into the mischiefs of desire and Satan. Contrary to this, those who do not value it, but rather constantly reject its guidance, within them it gradually, becoming weak, completely extinguishes, and that darkness covers them which leaves them completely deprived of insight and completely blind and deaf from the moral perspective. In the Quran, regarding such people, it is said: "No! Rather, the stain has covered their hearts of that which they were earning."

The Divine Light of the Inner Self: Joseph was among those who valued this light. The reward they received for it was that at this critical juncture, to which reference is made in this verse, this light saved him from getting surrounded by the darkness of desire and Satan. The words of the verse are clearly telling that as far as the woman is concerned, she had gone after him completely blind and deaf. Joseph too was a youth at that time. He was probably 18 or 20 years of age; it would not have been strange if his steps had also slipped. But no, within him was present that divine light whose guidance he had never rejected. It shone in his inner self on this occasion, and suddenly all darkness vanished from before his eyes. It is said: "Thus [it was] that We might avert from him evil and immorality. Indeed, he was of Our chosen servants." That is, since he was among Our chosen and selected servants whom We had selected for Our special work, for this reason, We guided him with Our proof at this critical juncture so as to protect him from evil and indecency. "Burhan" means a clear proof and decisive argument. What proof can be more clear and decisive than that which gives the call from within one's own inner self!

Some Aspects of the Protection (Ismah) of the Prophets: From this verse, some aspects of the protection of the Prophets are also illuminated. For example, one is that Allah Almighty protects them from the pollution of sins even prior to their commission of messengership. The second is that the meaning of the Prophet being protected is not that the power and capability to perform sin is snatched from him, but rather its meaning is that they constantly watch over their light of nature, because of which gradually it becomes so strong that even on occasions of severe trials, it does not let them deviate from the path.

And they both raced to the door, and she tore his shirt from the back, and they met her husband at the door. She said, "What is the recompense of one who intended evil with your wife except that he be imprisoned or a painful punishment?"

"Istibaq" means trying to exceed each other in running.

The Deceit of the Woman: When Joseph saw that no way of saving his life from this mischief remained, he rushed to the door so as to open it and go out. From behind, the woman pursued. She could not catch him indeed, but his shirt came into her hand. When she pulled it with force, it tore, and at the same time this incident occurred: when the door opened, they saw that her husband was standing by the door. Seeing him, all intoxication of love flew away. Immediately she spoke: what can be the recompense of the one who intends evil with your wife except that he be sent to prison or that he be given some painful punishment? In this way, she tried to prove herself innocent and Joseph a criminal in the eyes of her husband.

26–28. He said, "She seduced me." And a witness from her family testified. "If his shirt was torn from the front, then she has told the truth, and he is of the liars. But if his shirt was torn from the back, then she has lied, and he is of the truthful." So when [her husband] saw his shirt torn from the back, he said, "Indeed, it is of the conspiracy of you women. Indeed, your conspiracy is grand."

The Testimony of the Quran: When she tried to accuse Joseph, he expressed the actual reality in brief words: there is no fault of mine in this; rather, she is the one who was after me. It appears that the news of this event spread in the family too, and this too became known to people: that in this struggle, Joseph's shirt tore. As it were, a case was formed. The woman's statement was one thing, Joseph's statement another. Now, who is the liar and who is the truthful? In deciding this, if anything among the circumstances and context could be helpful, it was the event of the shirt tearing. One of the men of the woman's family, who was probably among the elders of the family, gave this opinion: if the shirt is torn from the front, then the woman is truthful and Joseph is lying; and if the shirt is torn from the back, then Joseph is truthful and the woman is lying. This point was highly reasonable; from this, the husband was reassured. When he saw that Joseph's shirt was torn from the back, he scolded the woman: this is all your deceit, and your deceit is highly dangerous. In "of the conspiracy of you women" (min kaydikunna), the plural pronoun expresses the intensity and severity of the man's anger. It is as if her action made not merely her alone, but her entire gender, deceitful and hated in his eyes.

"Joseph, ignore this. And, [O wife], ask forgiveness for your sin. Indeed, you were of the sinful."

The Husband's Scolding to the Wife: After the correct situation came before him, addressing Joseph, he reassured him: do not care for her nonsense. Ignore this. And addressing the woman, he scolded her: you alone are the guilty one, for this reason seek forgiveness for your sin. This matter should be kept in mind here: repentance and seeking forgiveness have always held great importance in polytheistic religions too; it is a separate matter that their seeking of forgiveness is before their deities who have no reality.

And women in the city said, "The wife of the Al-Aziz is seducing her slave; he has impassioned her with love. Indeed, we see her in clear error."

The Meaning of "Aziz": "Aziz" means predominant and powerful. This word is used for the king, and also for high-ranking, powerful officials. Since this person was the chief officer of the royal bodyguard, the women used the word "Al-Aziz" for him.

The Talk among the Women of the City: Gradually, the story of the love of Al-Aziz's wife for Joseph became the story of assemblies. Some of the women of the city, who could be of the same class as the Al-Aziz's wife, started saying: the wife of Al-Aziz is going mad in the love of her slave, she is throwing traps on him, but is unable to do anything. We see her in a clear error. In their this final sentence, many aspects of blame, malice, and claims are implicit. Their meaning was: firstly, this very thing is highly strange—that being the wife of a high official, she should disgrace herself behind her slave; then, an even stranger event—that she cannot even tame him. From here, within this blame, this theme also arises: this woman is foolish, in that she became disgraced and remained unsuccessful; if we had been there, in a single glance we would have given Joseph such a fall that all the fame of his piety would have ended.

So when she heard of their sly talk, she sent for them and prepared for them a banquet and gave each one of them a knife and said [to Joseph], "Come out before them." And when they saw him, they greatly admired him and cut their hands and said, "Perfect is Allah! This is not a man; this is none but a noble angel."

"So when she heard of their sly talk"—that is, when Al-Aziz's wife heard that these women have this pride in their beauty and their performances, her heart felt a great blow, and she wanted that they too try their skills on Joseph so that they too estimate their own value. Accordingly, she arranged an entertainment for them, prepared a sitting place for them with cushions according to the civilization of Egypt at that time, and when they came, she also put knives in their hands to eat fruits etc. After making this preparation, she said to Joseph: come out before them for a moment.

The Deceit of the Women and Their Failure: "And when they saw him, they greatly admired him and cut their hands and said, 'Perfect is Allah! This is not a man; this is none but a noble angel.'" "Ikbar" means to consider someone highly great and to believe in his greatness in the heart—that is, when Joseph came before them, seeing his divine form, his brilliant forehead, and his pure face, the women were stunned. The purity of character is in itself highly attractive, and when this is within a young man who is also handsome, his attraction becomes double. This very experience befell these women. They had come carrying the concept of a young slave to try their tricks on him. Here they saw that a holy form of purity and sanctity was standing before them. Seeing this, they estimated that on this angel-like character, all their arrows are useless; but since they had come with great claims, to do something was also necessary. Accordingly, the context tells and the testimonies of the Quran support that they tried to incline Joseph with their words, but when they saw that their words were not working on him at all, to create sympathy for themselves in his heart, they gave the threat of suicide, and some of them, to prove this threat true, as an initial step, injured their hands with the fruit-knives so that Joseph should not consider it a mere threat but fear that if he did not accept their word, some of them would surely destroy themselves. This matter should be kept in mind: the threat of suicide is among the highly effective weapons of a woman. When she does not see her weapons of beauty working on a man, this is the final weapon she tries. These women did the same.

This which we have submitted is not merely our conjecture. Ahead in this Surah, explicit statement of this is present: that these women tried to seduce Joseph on this occasion, and explicit statement is also present: that this matter of injuring the hands was a conspiracy (kayd) of these women. See: "And when the messenger came to him, [Joseph] said, 'Return to your master and ask him what is the case of the women who cut their hands. Indeed, my Lord is Knowing of their conspiracy.' [The king] said [to the women], 'What was your condition when you seduced Joseph?'": when the envoy came to him, he replied: return to your master and ask him what is the state of those women who had injured their hands. Indeed, my Lord is well aware of their conspiracy. He asked them: what was your condition when you tried to seduce Joseph.

The Threat of Suicide as a Weapon: Ponder: if the nature of the event had been that the women, being beside themselves by Joseph's beauty, had cut their hands, what aspect of a conspiracy was in it that Joseph would express it by "kayd"? And if they had not tried to trap Joseph in their net, why would this question be put to them: "What was your condition when you seduced Joseph?" It is possible a question arises in someone's mind: if the women had spoken some words to win Joseph's heart, why did the Quran not mention them? The answer to this question from our side is: to engage in such absurdities is not worthy of the dignity of the Quran, which is why in two words it compressed this entire record—that they tried their tricks on Joseph, so they estimated that to win him over with words was difficult, eventually they showed the display of the threat of suicide, but this threat also remained ineffective.

The Final Weapon of the Weak: The threat of suicide is the final weapon of the weak. Once I was sitting at the shop of a friend in Bombay. In the meantime, a beggar came and sat on the pavement in front of the shop. The owner of the shop threw a coin before him. He said: "Seth, I will not take less than five rupees." The Seth did not care for his word. He remained engaged in talking to me completely unconcerned. After boasting of his virtue for some time, he again demanded five rupees, and also made heard this threat: if five rupees were not given, he would burn himself to death right here. The Seth, even after hearing this threat, did not budge. He remained engaged in talking to me as before. Eventually, I saw that he lit a match and set fire to his trousers, and they started burning. Seeing this scene, I started sweating. I wanted to fulfill his demand from my pocket to end this story, but the Seth was not agreeable to this either. When his trousers burned up to near the knee, and he did not see the Seth melting, he quickly extinguished the fire with his own hands and walked away. The Seth said: we deal with such cheats daily. I said: these all seem to be the brothers of the women of Egypt.

The Meaning of "Hasha lillah": "Hasha lillah! This is not a man; this is none but a noble angel." "Hasha lillah" is a word of exemption and sanctification. This is spoken on the occasion when one wants to prove oneself or someone else free of some blame or accusation. In "ma hadha basharan" (this is not a man), "ma" is in the sense of "laysa" (is not), because of which "basharan" is in the accusative case (mansoob). Its parallel is present in the Quran.

The Admission of the Women: This admission of these women is the highest praise of Joseph, and also an excuse for their own defeat. Within it, this theme is also implicit: if we could not win him, the reason is not that there was any deficiency in our art or our beauty, but rather that we had to compete with an honorable angel, whereas all our weapons possess the capability to work only on humans.

She said, "That is the one about whom you blamed me. And I certainly demanded of him himself, but he firmly saved himself. And if he does not do what I command him, he will surely be imprisoned and will be of those debased."

Zulaikha's Threat: With the defeat of these women, the grief of Zulaikha's own defeat departed from her heart. In a highly sharp manner, she said to those women: this is the one about whom you had made me the target of blame and irony. Now you have estimated that to win him is no easy task! There is no doubt that I tried to take his heart in my fist, but he saved himself. But I say plainly: if he does not do what I command him, he will surely go to prison and will be disgraced. The meaning is: if I could not tame him through love, let him not think that his life has been delivered. Now he will eat the air of prison and will be disgraced. From here, Joseph's trial takes a new form. The love of lust prepares to take revenge for its failure, and it appears that in Egypt, masters possessed unlimited power over slaves. They could send them to prison without hesitation in the event of being angry with them. Then, here the matter was of the wife of a high government official. Who had the courage to oppose her intention.

He said, "My Lord, prison is more beloved to me than that to which they invite me. And if You do not avert from me their conspiracy, I will incline toward them and be of the ignorant."

Joseph's Prayer: This threat was given by Zulaikha to overawe Joseph—that fearing it, he would become ready to fulfill her desire; but Joseph, in front of these traps of lust, considered the chains of prison highly preferable. He immediately raised his hands for prayer: O Sustainer! In front of their invitation to lust, this prison is far more beloved and dear to me; and at the same time, he also spoke this sentence of pampering and trust: if You do not save me from their these mischiefs now, I will incline towards them and be overcome by emotions.

So his Lord answered him and averted from him their conspiracy. Indeed, He is the Hearing, the Knowing.

Acceptance of the Prayer: This prayer came out of Joseph's tongue at the time when he had completely squeezed his power for the protection of his faith and character. To the extent that in front of the mischievous life prepared by these women, he was agreeable to accepting the laborious life of prison. When a servant, after showing his steadfastness to this extent, casts himself before his Lord and seeks help from Him, his prayer is surely accepted, and accepted immediately. Accordingly, Joseph's prayer was also accepted, and he received deliverance from these feminine traps of Satan. "Indeed, He is the Hearing, the Knowing"—God is Hearing and Knowing. He hears the prayers and cries of the servant, and is well aware of the states and secrets of the hearts.

The Subject Ahead. Verses 35 to 53: A New Era of Joseph's Trial: From here, the new era of Joseph's trial begins. Although the actual reality was entirely clear to Al-Aziz and also to Zulaikha's relatives, they considered it beneficial to send Joseph to prison for some days. They must have thought: in this way, people's tongues will also stop after some time, and by being far from the eyes, Zulaikha's obsession will also depart. In prison, two companions of Joseph see dreams. They ask Joseph for the interpretation of the dreams. For Joseph, the way to tell the interpretation of dreams opens, after which the king becomes such an admirer of his that he makes him the master of all affairs of the country. Recite the verses.

"Then it occurred to them after they had seen the signs that they should surely imprison him for a time."

The Era of Trial of Prison: By "signs" (ayat) here is meant the proofs and testimonies of Joseph's innocence. That is, although this reality had become clear to Al-Aziz and his relatives—that Joseph is entirely innocent, the entire fault is Zulaikha's—to save their nose, they saw benefit only in sending Joseph to prison for some time. They must have thought: in this way, after some time, the obsession of Joseph will also depart from Zulaikha's mind, and the attention of those who are taking interest in this story will also deviate. People will think the fault was not Zulaikha's but Joseph's, which is why he received the punishment. In that era, in the presence of the unlimited powers which masters possessed over their slaves, for Al-Aziz, sending Joseph to prison would have had no trouble at first, and if there had been any, for a government official who was the chief officer of the bodyguard, what difficulty was there in removing such trouble. In short, from here, the new era of Joseph's trial began. Thus, in the common eye, the primary era of trial is this very one, but for Joseph, since the primary importance belonged to the protection of his faith and character, in front of the previous era, as you have seen, he not only desired this, but also prayed for it.

And there entered the prison with him two young men. One of them said, "Indeed, I saw myself pressing wine." And the other said, "Indeed, I saw myself carrying upon my head bread, from which the birds were eating. Inform us of its interpretation; indeed, we see you to be of those who do good."

"Fityan" means two young men; it is not explicitly stated in the Quran who the two young men were. If you believe the narration of the Torah, it is known that one of them was the king's cupbearer, the second the baker. "Indeed, I saw myself pressing wine"—this style is present in the Arabic language: some things are expressed from the perspective of their end. If the purpose of pressing grapes is to make wine from them, it can be said thus: I am pressing wine.

The Dreams of the Prisoner Young Men: Along with Joseph, two other young men, who were special servants of the king, entered the prison. Both saw dreams. One saw: I am pressing grapes. The second saw: I am carrying a basket of bread on my head and birds are plucking and eating from it. Since these dreams were highly different from common dreams, they became concerned to know their interpretation. During this time, they had had the opportunity to see Joseph from highly close, and they were influenced by his knowledge and piety. Naturally, for this work, they turned to him and requested with great respect: since you seem to us to be a highly good and pious man, for this reason, our request to you is to tell us the interpretation of the dreams. The word "muhsin" (one who does good) here is in the sense of a beautiful-doer—that is, whichever work he performs, he performs it keeping his Lord present and watching.

He said, "There will not come to you food provided for you but that I will have informed you of its interpretation before it comes to you. That is from what my Lord has taught me. Indeed, I have left the religion of a people who do not believe in Allah, and they, in the Hereafter, are disbelievers."

The Interpretation of the Dream and the Call to Religion: Joseph said: before the food is distributed, I will tell you the interpretation of these dreams. In the sad and uniform life of prison, especially of that era, the distribution of food etc. could be one of the few events which were considered a sign of change in life, which is why Joseph, referencing that, postponed the matter of the interpretation of dreams for some time. Its effect on those asking would be that Joseph has given importance to their dreams. He does not want to dismiss them by telling some random point, but rather, after thinking or turning to his Lord, he will tell their interpretation; and on this side, Joseph, by this postponement, created a highly excellent opportunity for the preaching of that truth which had become the most beloved objective of his life. When he saw two hearts turning towards him, he did not remain content merely with their blind devotion, but wanted to convey to them that call to the worship of Allah which, if they accepted, would beautify both their world and Hereafter. Accordingly, he first said: "That is from what my Lord has taught me"—this knowledge of the interpretation of dreams is among those knowledges which my Lord has taught me. The objective: if you consider this knowledge as a valuable thing, remember that its source and spring is not me, but my Lord. Among the knowledges He has granted me, this is one of them. The second point he said: "Indeed, I have left the religion of a people who do not believe in Allah"—that is, this favor of my Lord upon me occurred because I left the religion of those who do not believe in Allah. The third point he said: "and they, in the Hereafter, are disbelievers"—that is, they are the ones who are the deniers of the Hereafter, because for those who do not believe in God at all, the question of believing in the Hereafter does not arise. If you ponder, it will be known that as soon as Joseph felt that within these young men was some capability, he sowed the seeds of all the fundamental points of religion in them, and at the same time gave them the impression: if there is the courage to adopt this path, for this, the religion of those who do not believe in God and the Hereafter must be left.

Joseph's statement that: "I have left the religion of a people who do not believe in Allah", is the expression of the reality that he, by analyzing and evaluating in his inner self, had determined the truth to be truth and falsehood to be falsehood, and his adopting the truth was not merely because it was the heritage of his fathers, but because it was the thing to be adopted; similarly, if he had left falsehood, he had left it because its being falsehood had become completely clear.

"And I have followed the religion of my fathers, Abraham, Isaac and Jacob. It was not for us to associate anything with Allah. That is from the favor of Allah upon us and upon the people, but most of the people are not grateful."

Leaving the religion of those who do not believe in Allah, this is the description of the religion he adopted. The explanation of this entire golden chain was necessary because the addressees might otherwise fall into the misunderstanding that the religion to which Joseph is inviting them is some newly-invented religion of his; rather, the reality should become completely clear to them that this is the same religion which was adopted by such giants and famous figures as Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob (peace be upon them). The names of these giants were spread in all horizons, and it is not strange if Joseph's companions in prison were also familiar with these names.

The Historical and Natural Greatness of the Religion of Monotheism: "It was not for us to associate anything with Allah"—after clarifying the historical greatness of the religion of monotheism, Joseph has clarified its natural and rational greatness: this reality is as bright as the day—that there is a Creator who made this world, who granted us life, who provides sustenance, and in whose choice is our life and death; but we do not possess even the slightest testimony that anyone else is His partner and associate in these works. So when we do not possess even the slightest testimony in its favor—neither in our reason, nor in our nature, nor in the horizons, nor in our souls—how then does it belong to us to make something His partner and associate in His divinity without cause?

39–40. "O [my] two companions of the prison, are separate lords better or Allah, the One, the All-Conquering? You worship not besides Him except [mere] names which you have named, you and your fathers, for which Allah has sent down no authority. Legislation is not but for Allah. He has commanded that you worship not except Him. That is the correct religion, but most of the people do not know."

The Call to Monotheism: In the address of "O two companions of the prison", the eloquence and heart-pleasing nature are completely clear. Often, the sharing of a calamity also awakens the emotions of sympathy, love, sincerity, and mutual well-wishing in the hearts of the partners of calamity with great force and power. Joseph, by this address, has awakened those very emotions of his companions so that they turn towards him and listen to his words, which are entirely for their own benefit, with the ear of the heart. "are separate lords better or Allah, the One, the All-Conquering?"—the correct concept of "Al-Qahhar" (the All-Conquering) is of the one who is the Controller—that is, predominant and powerful over all, from whose grasp of authority no one is outside, nor can go outside. The meaning is: as far as one God is concerned, He is a self-evident reality, rather the most self-evident of realities. The polytheist and the monotheist both believe in Him. Now, as for the question of whether there are others besides Him as His partners, this thing is in need of highly strong proof. If no proof of it is present, based on mere desire, to make someone a god over oneself and let him dominate, where is the wisdom? If a question is placed before a slave: does he prefer the slavery of one master or of multiple masters simultaneously? He will prefer the slavery of one master in front of multiple masters. This is the nature of man. He has no interest in making someone a god over himself and letting him dominate; he believes in one God because his reason and nature compel him to believe in Him. What is the compulsion to believe in others besides Him—that he should put the collar of their slavery also in his neck. The Quran has pointed to this very reality of human nature in the parable of Surah Az-Zumar: "Allah presents an example: a man owned by quarreling partners and another belonging exclusively to one man. Are they equal in comparison?" Here, by the attribute of "Al-Qahhar", the objective is to point to the reality: when God is all-conquering as well—meaning predominant over all, and this is also the essential demand of His attributes—after all, what is the need for which others should be associated in His divinity?

"You worship not besides Him except names..."—that is, those whom you are worshipping are mere names invented by your illusion; their names have no named entities existing. The line which your fathers kept beating, without understanding, you too are beating that same line. God has not sent down any proof in favor of these imaginary goddesses and gods of yours. Decision and judgment, command and decree, belong only to Allah, and He has commanded only His worship, and this is the straight, upright, and natural religion. Other than this, all ways are deviated from the nature of man and are those which lose man in crooked paths; but the majority of people, due to their wretched deeds, are unaware of this reality.

"O [my] two companions of the prison, as for one of you, he will give his master wine to drink; and as for the other, he will be crucified, and the birds will eat from his head. The matter has been decided about which you both inquire."

The Interpretation of the Dreams: Since the call to Allah's religion is what dominates and predominates the hearts and minds of the Prophets, whenever and wherever they find an opportunity for it, they take advantage of it. When Joseph saw that his companions in prison had turned towards him with a good opinion and trust, first he conveyed to them the call to the monotheism of God, after which he also told them the interpretation of their dreams. He said: one of you, who saw the dream of pressing wine, will receive deliverance from the prison and will perform the service of cupbearing for his master. As for the second, who saw birds plucking and eating from the basket of bread on his head, he will be crucified and birds will pluck and eat from his head. He said: this is the interpretation of those dreams of yours about which you had asked me. I have fulfilled my promise.

And he said to the one whom he knew would go free of them, "Mention me before your master." But Satan made him forget the mention [of him] to his master, so Joseph remained in prison several years.

Permissible Device for a Permissible Objective: "And he said to the one whom he knew would go free of them, 'Mention me before your master.'" To one of the two, regarding whom Joseph had the assumption based on his dream that he would be released from the prison, Joseph made this desire: if some appropriate opportunity arises, mention me before your master. By "master" (rabb) here is meant the king, because he was, as passed above, the king's cupbearer. By "mention" (dhikr) is obviously meant the mention of those things which had come to his knowledge and observation during this companionship in prison, specifically the mention of the interpretation of the dream which was an important event and such matters keep coming before the courts of kings daily. Joseph would have expressed this desire in the hope that, where all doors of appeal and justice are closed, perhaps from this path some way of release from this oppressed imprisonment would appear. To adopt permissible devices and means for a permissible objective is not contrary to reliance and trust upon Allah. Contrary to reliance is that a person, being broken-hearted by circumstances, resorts to adopting impermissible devices. The servant accepted in the sight of Allah Almighty is not the one who sits with broken hands and feet in the name of reliance, but rather the one who steps into the battlefield of life, makes use of means and devices, and uses causes and sources, but at every step respects the limits and boundaries of God. This is the primary trial for which God created the creation, and which is the purpose of the existence of this world.

The Decision of the Divine Decree: "But Satan made him forget the mention to his master, so Joseph remained in prison several years." The servant released from the prison forgot to make mention of Joseph before his master—namely, the king. This forgetting was attributed to Satan because to make one unmindful of a good deed is the work of Satan alone. From this, it was known that to adopt devices and means is among the duties of man, but the bringing to work of these devices and means is dependent on the decree of Allah and His wisdom. The demand of Allah Almighty's wisdom and interest was that Joseph should still spend some more years in the prison. Accordingly, this was fulfilled in such a way that Joseph's companion released from the prison, coming out of the prison, could not remember at all what the companion of prison had said to him.

And [one day] the king said, "Indeed, I saw [in a dream] seven fat cows being eaten by seven lean ones, and seven green spikes [of grain] and others [that were] dry. O eminent ones, explain to me my vision, if you should be of visions interpreters."

The King's Dream: When Allah wants a thing, no matter how far-fetched it may be, its causes begin to appear on their own. Accordingly, during this time, the king himself saw a dream: there are seven fat cows, and seven lean ones, and these lean cows are swallowing the fat cows. Similarly, there are seven green spikes of grain and other seven dry ones, and these seven dry spikes are eating the seven green spikes. The king felt this dream to be of great importance, which is why he related this dream to his courtiers and said to them: if you interpret dreams, tell me the interpretation of this dream of mine. From this final sentence of the king, this point is expressed: in his view, this dream was of great importance, and he was also feeling that telling its interpretation was no easy task for these court-interpreters.

In the verse, after "and others dry" (wa ukhara yabisaat), the word "ya'kulna" (eating) is omitted. Due to the presence of the context, it was omitted. Ahead in verse 48, its clarification has occurred. We have opened it in the translation.

They said, "[It is but] a mixture of false dreams, and we are not, concerning the interpretation of [such] dreams, knowing."

The Response of the Courtiers: "Dhighth" refers to a bundle of twigs and grass. Its plural is "adghaath", which is also used for a collection of worthless things and reports. Then, from this, the compound "adghaathu ahlaam" (muddled dreams) arose, by which is meant those confused dreams which appear just like that due to stomach gas or some other cause; there is no interpretation in them.

The interpreters of the court, by speaking this point, first tried to remove the king's anxiety—that: Your Majesty should not worry much about this dream, this is a thing of the nature of muddled dreams, there is no interpretation in such dreams; and at the same time tried to maintain their own prestige—that: our job is to tell the interpretation of meaningful dreams, not of muddled dreams.

And the one who was freed of the two remembered after a period of time and said, "I will inform you of its interpretation, so send me forth."

"Iddakara", "iddadakara", and "idh-dhakara" are all used in the same meaning, and "ummah" here means a period and duration of time.

The Offer of the Royal Cupbearer: The helplessness of the court-interpreters in front of the interpretation of this dream reminded the royal cupbearer, who had been released from the prison, of the story of his dream after a period of time, the interpretation of which Joseph had told and which had turned out entirely correct. He immediately stepped forward and said: I will tell you the interpretation of this dream, so allow me to go to meet the one whom I want to meet for this purpose.

[He said], "Joseph, O man of truth, explain to us seven fat cows eaten by seven lean ones, and seven green spikes [of grain] and others [that were] dry—that I may return to the people, perhaps they will know."

Joseph Requested by the Cupbearer: Here, so much of the narration was omitted due to the clarity of the context: eventually the courtiers allowed the cupbearer to go to Joseph, and he arrived in the prison in his presence. As soon as he arrived, he addressed Joseph with "Ayyuha-as-Siddieq" (O man of truth), the meaning of which is: O upright, O personification of truth, O truth incarnate. From this, it is known: he had not become his admirer merely due to the interpretation of his dream, but rather during the companionship in prison for a period of time, the experience of Joseph's pure life and truth had made him his admirer. Accordingly, instead of addressing him with "O teller of the interpretation of dreams", he addressed him with "O man of truth", which is a comprehensive and perfect expression of being characterized with all beautiful attributes.

After this address, he mentioned the dream: there are seven lean cows which are swallowing seven fat cows, and seven dry spikes of grain which are eating seven green spikes of grain. Along with asking the interpretation of the dream, he also made mention of the point that outside, people are anxious in the matter of this dream, for this reason, tell me the interpretation of this dream as quickly as possible so that I can go outside and tell people and they are reassured. Within "perhaps they will know" (la'allahum ya'lamoon), there is a highly subtle allusion to the point that: people are merely throwing arrows in the dark, but what you tell will be based on correct knowledge and from it people will obtain guidance.

47–49. [Joseph] said, "You will sow for seven years consecutively; and what you harvest leave in its spikes, except a little from which you will eat. Then will come after that seven difficult [years] which will consume what you saved for them, except a little from which you will store. Then will come after that a year in which the people will be given rain and in which they will press [olives and grapes]."

"Tazra'oona" (you will sow) is indeed in the form of a report, but it is in the concept of a command. We have clarified in another place: when the occasion is of guiding, advising, and instructing, in such situations, instead of a command, the style of a report remains appropriate. Parallels of this are many in the Quran.

The Interpretation of the Dream: Joseph told the interpretation of the dream, and along with it, also guided towards the device to meet that seven-year dreadful famine of which this dream had informed. He said: sow consecutively for seven years, so what you harvest, except for the portion of food requirement which you separate and clean, leave all the remaining grain in its spikes so that it remains preserved. This point should be kept in mind here: when grains remain within the chaff and spikes, they remain safe from the calamity of termites, weevils, and insects.

After that, he told the interpretation of the second part of the dream: seven years of consecutive, highly severe famine will come which will eat up all that store which you will gather for the famine. Out of this preserved store, you will be able to save only a very small part. Along with this, he also made heard the glad tidings: after that, such a year will come in which people's cries will be answered—that is, the cries, prayers, and wails of people will find hearing with God, His cloud of mercy will shower, the harvest of grapes will be highly fruitful, and people will press grapes.

By "yughaathu" (they will be given rain), some people have also interpreted rain, but in my view, rain is not its literal meaning, though as its necessary meaning, it can be included in its concept. A clear benefit of taking the word in its literal meaning is that the complete picture of its all-encompassing effect comes forward—that all people will cry out due to its effect and every person will weep and grovel before his Lord. This point should be kept in mind: when a severe time practically falls, as is explicitly stated in the Quran, even the stanchest disbelievers and polytheists call upon God alone, forgetting their other imaginary goddesses and gods. Then, this period of famine, as details will follow, was spent entirely by the people of Egypt under the guidance of Joseph, in which from the king to the commoners, all were his admirers, obedient, and believers.

By the word "ya'siroon" (they will press), the objective is also to point to its necessary consequence. That is: rain will fall well, the vines of grapes will flourish well, and people will press grapes. Along with this, a subtle allusion has also arisen in it. It is that the asker, as passed above, was the king's special cupbearer. Due to this suitability, the word "ya'siroon" has created a special beauty in the speech, which is not hidden from those who possess a taste.

And the king said, "Bring him to me." But when the messenger came to him, [Joseph] said, "Return to your master and ask him what is the case of the women who cut their hands. Indeed, my Lord is Knowing of their conspiracy."

Joseph Invited by the King and His Response: Here too, so much of the narration is omitted as was clear from the context—namely, the king's envoy went and related the interpretation described by Joseph to the king. The king, hearing the interpretation of his dream, was so influenced that he immediately commanded: bring this prisoner to me. The envoy again went to Joseph and happily made him hear the glad tidings: the king has remembered you.
