The envoy would have expected that a prisoner, who has been suffering the hardships of prison for years, hearing this glad tidings of his release, would be beside himself with joy and would immediately accompany him; but Joseph, entirely contrary to his expectation, gave him this reply: return to your master (the king) and ask him what is the story of those women who had injured their hands? The meaning is: this event should be investigated, which was made an excuse to send me to prison. Thus, my Lord is well aware of their conspiracy, and for my reassurance His being aware is sufficient, but I want that before my release, this event should be investigated so that later, on its basis, no one can muster the courage to speak against me. If you go deep into this instruction of Joseph and ponder, this reality will be clear: he did not prefer his release and closeness to the king merely by taking advantage of the king's temporary good opinion, but rather gave the primary importance to the clearance of the accusation, and he had such trust in his truth and his Lord that he did not care in the least what lies the opposite party could spin to make him accused.

[The king] said [to the women], "What was your condition when you seduced Joseph?" They said, "Perfect is Allah! We know about him not any evil." The wife of the Al-Aziz said, "Now the truth has become manifest. It was I who seduced him, and indeed, he is of the truthful."

Investigation of the Event of the Women: Here too, on the basis of the clarity of the context, so much is omitted: the envoy conveyed Joseph's point to the king. Accordingly, the king summoned those women and asked them: what is your story when you tried to seduce Joseph? From the words of the king's question, this entire point is completely clear: at least in this stage, this reality had become completely clear to him—that this entire deceit was of these women, Joseph was entirely innocent in this matter. If this reality had not been clear to the king, the style of the question would have been different. That is, he would have asked: what is this event that befell you with Joseph? The truth is that truth demands patience for its manifestation. If some servant of Allah adopts it and performs the right of patience required for it, that time inevitably comes when the echo of his truth is heard from the doors and walls. To the extent that even the enemies, as you will see, bear witness to it.

"They said, 'Perfect is Allah! We know about him not any evil.'" "Hasha" and "hash" are words of exemption and sanctification, and are spoken when one wants to prove someone free of some blame or accusation.

The Admission of the Women: We have pointed above: the great patience of Joseph had turned the direction of circumstances in such a way that it had not remained possible for anyone to speak anything against him. Accordingly, these women also with highly open hearts admitted: we evaluated him well but did not find any trace of evil on him; and the Al-Aziz's wife herself, who had raised this entire mischief, went even further than these women in admitting the truth. She said: now the truth has become fully manifest, what is the benefit of hiding now; the actual reality is that I tried to trap him, but he remained completely safe. Undoubtedly, he is among the truthful.

52–53. [Joseph said], "That is so Al-Aziz may know that I did not betray him in [his] absence and that Allah does not guide the plan of betrayers. And I do not acquit myself. Indeed, the soul is a persistent enjoiner of evil, except those upon whom my Lord has mercy. Indeed, my Lord is Forgiving and Merciful."

The Reason for Demanding the Investigation: After his chest was proven clean of stains, Joseph also told the reason why he considered it necessary to initiate this case anew when the king was releasing him without discussing this matter. He said: I did so because it should become completely clear to the king that I was sent to prison completely innocent, I had not performed any faithlessness with Al-Aziz in his absence, and this reality should also become completely clear—that Allah Almighty does not make the plans of the faithless and the betrayers successful, but rather eventually they are unsuccessful, and those who remain upright on truth and loyalty are the ones who are successful.

In our view, the referent of the pronoun in "liyadh-haba" (so he may know) is the king, whose mention has occurred in highly clear words in the verses above, and the referent of the pronoun in "akhunahu" (I betray him) is Al-Aziz, for whom a clear context is present. If contexts are present, such distribution in pronouns is no defect.

After this, Joseph also made clear this reality: let no one fall into this misunderstanding from my this attitude—that I consider my soul above sins. The soul is with me too, and it also keeps whispering for evil. A man remains safe from its mischiefs only as long as God's mercy and His favor are accompanying. So if I remained safe from the mischiefs, I remained safe not because I did not possess a soul within me, but I remained safe only because my Lord's mercy and favor took my hand. My Lord is Forgiving and Merciful. In "except those upon whom my Lord has mercy" (illa ma rahima rabbi), "ma" in our view is adverbial (zarfiyyah).

A Divine Sunnah: Remember this divine sunnah here: Allah Almighty, inspiring man with goodness and evil, has given him the choice to adopt the path of evil or of goodness. If he adopts the path of goodness and faces the trials occurring in this path with determination, Allah Almighty turns towards him with mercy and favor and makes his path easy, until he reaches the destination; and if any person leaves the path of goodness and adopts the path of evil, Allah Almighty gives him leeway to progress in this path. Joseph adopted the high calls of the soul and faced all the oppositions of this path with full determination, until the low calls of the soul surrendered before him and Allah Almighty blessed him with that elevation of rank which was destined for him.

The Subject Ahead. Verses 54 to 101:

A New Era of Joseph's Life: Now ahead begins the new era of Joseph's (peace be upon him) life. He not only gets released from the prison, but practically the entire government of Egypt comes under his thumb. To meet the seven-year famine, he manages the circumstances with such beauty that he not only succeeds in fulfilling the food requirements of the country, but also helps the nearby countries in solving their difficulties. Those brothers of his, by whose favor he had reached the slavery of the Al-Aziz and eventually the prison, in this era of authority, they too come to his presence to acquire grain, though they do not know that this is their same brother whom they had thrown into a dark well. In their this ignorance, Joseph also tests them in some subtle ways so as to estimate whether, after doing all that they did with him, any change has occurred in their inner self. Eventually, that time comes when this reality is made clear to them: the one before whom they are present as askers is their same brother whom they had thrown into the well. After this, Joseph calls all his brothers and parents to him. When they come, they bow their heads in his court in respect, and in this way, Joseph's that dream is proven true which he had seen—that eleven stars and the sun and the moon are prostrating to me. Recite the verses.

"And the king said, 'Bring him to me; I will select him for myself.' And when he spoke to him, he said, 'Indeed, you are today with us established and secure.'"

The King's Admiration for Joseph: The king, firstly, became his admirer by this very point—that of the dream from whose interpretation all the wise and noble of his court fell short, he told such an interpretation as entered everyone's heart. Then, greater than this, the point that influenced him was that Joseph, along with the interpretation of the dream, also told the device to meet this dreadful famine, the occurrence of which the dream had informed.

Then, greater than both of these points, the third point was that when the king wanted to release him from the prison and call him to him, he refused to accept this offer until those accusations were investigated, which were made an excuse to send him to prison. The result of this investigation too, as is known from the verses above, emerged highly glorious in Joseph's favor.

Any single one of these three points could have been sufficient to make the king an admirer of Joseph, but the person regarding whom these three points collectively came to the king's knowledge, if he did not become his admirer without seeing him, what else would he do? Accordingly, he immediately commanded: let him be brought to me immediately, I will make him my special confidant. In personal governments, the center of all powers is the king's person, because of which, if he makes someone his special confidant—and that too with the admiration and devotion which had arisen in the king's heart for Joseph—its meaning is that the king announced the intention of handing over the rein of choice and authority to Joseph along with the announcement of his release.

The Offer of Rank to Joseph: "And when he spoke to him, he said, 'Indeed, you are today with us established and secure.'" Until now, other people were the medium between the king and Joseph. He had not had any opportunity to meet him directly and speak to him. Now when the meeting occurred, he also spoke to him. The Quran, although it has referred to this conversation briefly, obviously this conversation must have occurred in detail. Religious and moral issues would have been under discussion, and on the administrative and economic issues of the country too, Joseph must have presented his opinion. The correct estimate of a man's personality is made only by meeting and speaking. Accordingly, it appears that this meeting and speaking made the king a further admirer of Joseph, and he said to him: now you are a powerful confidant in our government. As it were, the king placed the offer of the greatest rank of his government before him, and left it to Joseph's discretion as to in what form he would accept this offer.

He said, "Appoint me over the storehouses of the land. Indeed, I will be a knowing guardian."

Joseph's Proposal: By "the land" (al-ard) here is meant the land of Egypt, and by "storehouses" (khaza'in) is meant the sources of production of the country. Since at that time, the greatest campaign was to manage the coming famine, for this reason, Joseph said: hand over the management and administration of all the sources of production of the country to me, I am a protector of everything with full care, and I also possess the knowledge to manage this responsibility. This should remain clear: this was not a petition from Joseph's side, but rather, to accept the king's own offer and bring it to work, whichever form he found appropriate, he placed it before him, and at the same time also manifested that the honesty and knowledge required to manage this responsibility, he finds within himself. The meaning is: for this reason, I expect to perform this work with beauty. This proposal of Joseph was exactly according to the king's desire, which is why he was reassured, and in this way, the kingdom of Egypt came, as it were, under the thumb of Joseph (peace be upon him). In the Torah, it says: then Pharaoh said to Joseph: see, I have given you rule over all the land of Egypt; and Pharaoh took off his ring from his hand and put it on Joseph's hand. Then it was proclaimed before him: let everyone stay with respect; and he made him ruler over all the land of Egypt. And he said to Joseph: I am Pharaoh, and without your command, no man shall lift his hand or foot in all the land of Egypt. (Genesis, 41: 44).

56–57. And thus We established Joseph in the land to settle therein wherever he willed. We touch with Our mercy whom We will, and We do not allow to be lost the reward of those who do good. And the reward of the Hereafter is better for those who believed and used to fear Allah.

The Absolute Authority of Joseph (peace be upon him): In this way, Joseph (peace be upon him) acquired practically all that choice and authority in the entire land of Egypt which the king possessed. The name indeed remained of the king, but he delegated all his choices to Joseph. "to settle therein wherever he willed"—that is, wherever he willed to reside, whichever part of the country he willed to tour, where he considered some administration necessary, he issued commands for it. It was the duty of the officials of the government to comply with his commands without questioning.

Allah's Dealing is with the Doers of Good: In "We touch with Our mercy whom We will, and We do not allow to be lost the reward of those who do good", two realities are made clear. One is that all choice and authority belong only to God. If He wants to bless someone with His favor and mercy, no one can hold His hand. When He wills, He takes out a prisoner from the prison and makes him sit on the throne of the kingdom. This is an ordinary miracle of His power and will. The second is that He does not allow to be lost the reward of the doers of good (muhsineen). Trials indeed befall them; if they remain upright and steadfast on the path of God in these trials, Allah Almighty rewards them in this world too, and in the Hereafter too they will receive its reward. "And the reward of the Hereafter is better for those who believed and used to fear Allah"—that is, the reward of the Hereafter is far greater than the reward of this world, which Allah has reserved for those who remain steadfast on faith and protect themselves from violating the limits and boundaries of God.

And the brothers of Joseph came [to Egypt], and they entered upon him; and he recognized them, but he was to them unknown.

The Presence of Joseph's Brothers: This coming of Joseph's (peace be upon him) brothers, as is clarified in the verse ahead, was for the purpose of acquiring grain. That is, this is the time of seven years later when Egypt and its adjacent countries, Palestine and Syria etc., have all come under the grip of famine. This famine was in Egypt too, but due to Joseph's foresight and his good administration, Egypt was not only successful in fulfilling its requirements, but there, the needy coming from nearby countries were also allowed to buy grain in a specified quantity from the government depots. Accordingly, for the purpose of acquiring grain, Joseph's brothers came to Egypt, and it appears that due to being foreigners, they had to appear before Joseph specifically to acquire the special permit. At the time of this appearance, Joseph recognized them, but these people could not recognize him. The reason for not recognizing is obvious: firstly, at the time when they had thrown him into the well, a period of more than twenty or twenty-one years had passed on it; in such a long time, the form and appearance of a man change much. Then, even if they had felt some glimpse of him in his face and appearance, as an allusion emerges ahead from verse 90, after all, how could they assume that the brother whom they had thrown into the dark well was today menez over the throne of the kingdom of Egypt.

59–60. And when he had furnished them with their supplies, he said, "Bring me a brother of yours from your father. Do you not see that I give full measure and that I am the best of hosts? But if you do not bring him to me, then there will be no measure for you from me, nor will you approach me."

Joseph's Instruction to His Brothers: After getting their supplies arranged, Joseph directed them: when you come to get grain next time, bring your stepbrother along too. It appears that due to the rationing system being in force, every needy received according to the members of the family, which is why they had to tell that: our one stepbrother is also at home. Joseph said: when you come next, bring him along too. For this, he gave them encouragement and also a threat. The encouragement: see, I give the grain of every individual fully. By "measure" (kayl) is meant the vessel of measuring, but just as by speaking the vessel, sometimes the content is meant, similarly by it here is meant the grain. The meaning: if you bring your brother, you will receive the grain of his share fully, and this experience you have already had—that I entertain those who come in an extremely good way. The threat he gave: if you do not bring your brother, then there is no grain for you with me, nor will you come near me.

They said, "We will attempt to dissuade his father of him, and indeed, we will do [it]."

The Brothers' Response: "Murawadah" means to attempt to dissuade or win over. They said: in his matter, we will try to make his father agree, and as far as our effort is concerned, we will leave no stone unturned. Within their this sentence, this point is implicit: if we do not succeed in bringing him, there will be no fault of ours in this; rather, its meaning is that his father did not allow him, and this is a thing in which our helplessness is clear. Due to what they had done with Joseph, there was a thief in their heart, which is prominent in their this sentence.

And [Joseph] said to his servants, "Put their merchandise into their saddlebags that they might recognize it when they have returned to their people that perhaps they will [then] return."

Further Kindness: Joseph (peace be upon him), with the thought that financial difficulties should not prevent them from coming again, did this: he commanded his men to put the money they had paid as the price of grain into their saddlebags so that when they opened their saddlebags at home, they would feel that this favor was done to them, and happily, with encouragement, they would come again to get grain.

63–64. So when they returned to their father, they said, "O our father, [further] measure has been denied to us, so send with us our brother that we may get measure. And indeed, we will be his guardians." He said, "Should I entrust you with him except as I entrusted you with his brother before? But Allah is the best guardian, and He is the most merciful of the merciful."

The Report of the Sons to Their Father and His Response: When these people reached home, they related the entire story to their father: in the future, we have been deprived of grain. Now, to acquire grain, there is only one way: we must take our brother Benjamin along with us, and prove ourselves true—that we have an eleventh brother too; so allow Benjamin to go with us this time so that we can get the grain of everyone's share, and we reassure you that we will protect him fully. Jacob said: in the matter of Benjamin, if I trust you, this trust will be like that which I made in the matter of his brother Joseph before. So Allah alone is the best protector, and He is the most merciful of the merciful. From Jacob's final words, this point emerges: as far as your trust is concerned, its experience I have already had; however, if Benjamin must be sent with you, I will do this work relying on Allah's protection and mercy, not on your trust.

And when they opened their baggage, they found their merchandise returned to them. They said, "O our father, what [more] should we desire? This, our merchandise has been returned to us. And we will obtain food for our family and protect our brother and obtain an increase of a camel's load. That is an easy measurement."

The Joy of the Sons: When these people opened their saddlebags and saw that the money they had paid as the price of grain was present fully in their sacks of grain, they leapt with joy. They said: O father! What more do we want, see that our money has also been returned to us. Now we will go, bring grain for our family, protect our brother, and acquire one camel-load of grain further; this grain which we brought is indeed small.

Be sacrificial to the eloquence of the Quran, in that it has related their word in such a way that their joy overflows from their every single sentence.

In "And we will obtain food for our family" (wa nameeru ahlana), the conjoined is omitted due to the clarity of the context; we have opened it in the translation. "Mara, yameeru, meera" means to arrange grain and necessary things for one's family. The meaning is: now that the money has been returned, what obstacle remains in our way, now we will surely go and bring grain for our family. From "a camel's load" (kayla ba'eer), this point was known: in the rationing system which Joseph (peace be upon him) had initiated, foreign needy were allowed to acquire one camel-load of grain.

[Jacob] said, "Never will I send him with you until you give me a solemn oath by Allah that you will surely bring him [back] to me, unless you should be surrounded [by enemy]." And when they had given him their solemn oath, he said, "Allah, over what we say, is Witness."

Jacob's (peace be upon him) Conditional Permission: Jacob (peace be upon him) said: I will allow Benjamin to go with you only in the event when you make a strong covenant with me in the name of Allah—that you will surely bring him back, unless you yourself are surrounded by some calamity. So when they swore and made the covenant with him, Jacob said: over this agreement we are making, Allah is Guardian and Witness.

And he said, "O my sons, do not enter from one gate but enter from different gates; and I cannot avail you against Allah at all. Decision is not but for Allah; upon Him I have relied, and upon Him let those who would rely rely."

"ma ughnee 'ankum" means: I cannot be of any use to you, I cannot cause you any benefit.

A Directing Device of Interest: Since it was the time of famine, in which the possibilities of theft, robbery, and other crimes of this nature increase much, for this reason, Jacob, while bidding them farewell, directed them: when you enter the city, do not enter from one single gate, but enter from different gates. This matter should be kept in mind: in ancient times, for protection from external attacks, there were walls around every city of note and specified gates for entry from every direction. Jacob would have given this direction probably with the fear: let these people not become the target of the attention of the city's wicked and ruffians. The entry of eleven dignified, handsome, and robust brothers with their caravan through a single gate at the same time could be an attention-grabbing thing, and it was also possible some wicked people would go after them thinking they must have much wealth, and in this greed, they would try to cause them harm.

Along with this direction, he made heard this warning: this is a device which, considering it good in my knowledge, I advise you to adopt. Do not take this device in the meaning that it can save you from that decree which God has written for you. That is irreversible at its place. The real choice belongs only to God. Upon Him I have relied, and those who rely should rely on Him.

And when they entered from where their father had commanded them, it did not avail them against Allah at all, except [that it was] a need within the soul of Jacob, which he satisfied. And indeed, he was a possessor of knowledge because of what We had taught him, but most of the people do not know.

The Relationship between Decree and Device: Now, the relationship between device and decree is clarified: the action which the sons performed regarding entering the city on their father's advice was merely a device keeping in view the circumstances of the time, the need for which was felt in the father's heart and he directed them to adopt it, which proved beneficial and blessed for them. Its meaning is not that if any calamity had been written in their decree, they would have been saved from it by this device too. The decree is irreversible, which no one's device can change. But despite this, it is the duty of man to adopt devices according to the circumstances and interests. In this lies the trial of his will, effort, and action, and on this depends his rank and status in the Hereafter. For this reason, it is not permissible for anyone to sit down with broken hands and feet in the name of the decree. Rather, it is necessary to adopt devices within the limits appointed by Allah, to make effort up to one's capability to bring them to work, and along with this, to believe that only that will happen which is in Allah's will. No one can change Allah's decision. "And indeed, he was a possessor of knowledge..."—this is the praise and appreciation of Jacob, in that he was blessed with that knowledge in the matter of device and decree which Allah had taught him; but most people are unaware of their reality. They either consider the device to be everything, the decree being an illusion to them, or they sit down completely paralyzed in the name of the decree and reliance.

And when they entered upon Joseph, he took his brother to himself; he said, "Indeed, I am your brother, so do not be distressed by what they have been doing."

Revealing the Secret to the Brother: When these people reached Joseph (peace be upon him), calling his brother Benjamin to himself in private, he informed him: I am your brother Joseph. So do not be distressed and grieved by what they have done so far. Now this era has passed. Context suggests that on this occasion, Joseph (peace be upon him) must have also informed his brother of that device which he was to adopt to stop him with himself, so that he would remain reassured in the circumstances to occur ahead.

Commentary of Verses 70 to 76: "So when he had furnished them with their supplies, he put the drinking cup into the saddlebag of his brother. Then an announcer called out, 'O caravan, indeed you are thieves.' They said, turning to them, 'What is it you are missing?' They said, 'We are missing the measure of the king. And for he who produces it is the load of a camel, and I am for it responsible.' They said, 'By Allah, you have certainly known that we did not come to cause corruption in the land, and we have not been thieves.' They said, 'So what is its recompense if you should be liars?' They said, 'Its recompense is that he in whose saddlebag it is found—he is its recompense. Thus we recompense the wrongdoers.' So he began with their bags before the bag of his brother; then he extracted it from the bag of his brother. Thus We planned for Joseph. He could not have taken his brother within the law of the king except that Allah willed. We raise in degrees whom We will, and over every possessor of knowledge is one knowing."

Joseph's Device to Stop His Brother: When their supplies were arranged, the water-cup, which was used as the royal vessel to measure grain, was put by Joseph into the saddlebag of his brother. Then an announcer called out: O caravan-people, you are thieves. Rushing, they turned towards them and asked: what is it you are missing? They said: the royal vessel is missing; so whoever brings it will get one camel-load of grain as a reward, and I am responsible for it. They swore: you know well that we did not come to spread corruption in this land, and we are not thieves. They asked: if you are proven liars, what is the punishment of a thief? They said: the one in whose saddlebag the vessel is found, he himself should be stopped in its place. We recompense the wrongdoers in this way. After this, Joseph took the search of their baggage, and initiated the search from their bags before his brother's bag, and in the end, extracted the vessel from his brother's bag. Regarding this device of Joseph, Allah's statement is: We made this device for Joseph. By the law of the king, he was not authorized to stop his brother, unless Allah willed. It is said: We raise the ranks of whom We will, and over every owner of knowledge is a possessor of knowledge.

Dispelling Doubts regarding Joseph's (peace be upon him) Performance: This is the summary of these verses which we have described in our own words. Now we will submit a few fundamental points in a sequence so that the doubts arising in the mind regarding this performance of Joseph are cleared:

1. The first point is that Joseph did not want to reveal himself to his brothers at this stage, but rather wanted to evaluate them well to see whether, after performing such a great injustice as they did with him, any change had occurred in their inner self, or their way of thinking was still the same as before.

2. The second point is that Joseph (peace be upon him), after finding his brother Benjamin, was not ready under any cost to take the risk of handing him over to these unjust people. He would have thought: although they brought him in this greed because they knew that without him there was no possibility of getting grain, what is far from such people on their return that they would perform some such deed with him too as they had performed with himself. After all, the emotion of jealousy which was the motive of their first action is present in this brother's case too.

3. The third point is that he had to respect the law of the land too. There is no doubt that due to the king's extraordinary admiration, Joseph (peace be upon him) possessed all kinds of choices, as passed behind; but what was worthy of his dignity was that whichever step he took should be according to the law of the land, especially when that law was based on justice too.

4. The fourth point is that to emerge successful from these diverse circumstances, if any method could be effective, it could be only the method of "tawriyah" (allusion/subtlety). If tawriyah is for some false objective and contains the mixture of lies too, then such tawriyah is undoubtedly prohibited; but if it is for some true objective and is free of lies and deceit, then not only is there no defect in this tawriyah, but sometimes its adoption, especially against the enemy, becomes necessary for the interest of truth. Some highly subtle and pure examples of this are found in the life of our Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) too. In this kind of tawriyah, the thing said or done is true in itself, but the style of saying or doing it is such that the addressee falls into a misunderstanding.

5. Joseph putting the vessel in the brother's bag, and making the announcer announce that: O caravan-people, you are thieves; the announcer made this announcement complying with Joseph's command, and the point which Joseph kept in view while making this announcement was not that the caravan-people had stolen the royal vessel, but rather he kept his own event in view—that they took him from home in the name of recreation, threw him into a well, and on returning home in the evening, tried to make their old father believe that a wolf ate Joseph. Accordingly, see that the announcer's words are not: "O caravan-people, you have stolen the royal vessel"; rather, his words are: "O caravan-people, you are thieves." Obviously, this point was entirely correct in itself, though on this occasion, it could naturally create the misunderstanding that some specific theft was the cause of this announcement.

6. Along with this announcement, it appears that it spread in Joseph's staff that the royal vessel was missing. Since this royal vessel was originally the king's cup, it must have been valuable, because of which its being stolen was not considered far-fetched. It is possible that Joseph himself mentioned the missing of the royal vessel, and his staff on their own formed the opinion that the theft of which the caravan-people are accused is the theft of the royal vessel.

7. When the caravan-people turned and asked Joseph's men: what is it you are missing? They replied according to their knowledge: our royal vessel is missing; and along with it, one of them also said: whoever brings the vessel will get one camel-load of grain as a reward, and I am responsible for it. This final point was obviously spoken at Joseph's instance, and most likely, this was spoken by the same man who had made the announcement: O caravan-people, you are thieves.

8. The caravan-people first swore and presented their defense—that they had not come to spread corruption in this land, and they were not thieves; then, when they were asked: if you are proven liars, what is the punishment of a thief? They replied with a brief pause: the one in whose bag the thing is found, he himself should be stopped in its place. We recompense the wrongdoers in this way.

9. After this, Joseph himself took the search, and initiated the search from others before Benjamin so that they would have no doubt, and in the end, extracted the royal vessel from Benjamin's bag. In this way, they became authorized to stop their brother by the law of the land too, and by their brothers' own admission too.

10. This device, Allah Almighty describes as "kayd" (plan) and attributes this "kayd" to Himself. "Kayd" means a secret plan. This secret plan was taught by Allah Almighty to Joseph, by virtue of which he became capable of protecting his brother from danger and also maintaining the respect of the law of the land.

In all this detail, if you ponder, it will be known that in all this performance, neither Joseph (peace be upon him) was involved in any lie, nor his men. Temporarily indeed, a stain of accusation fell upon Benjamin, but firstly, he was, as we have indicated, informed of it beforehand; secondly, this was done only to save him from the oppression of his stepbrothers.

At the end, in "We raise in degrees whom We will, and over every possessor of knowledge is one knowing", there is an allusion to Joseph's high ranks, and also a criticism against those who consider their own knowledge to be the final thing. To the extent they see the world, they think this is all of the universe, and in this intoxication, they mock many realities which are outside their limited knowledge. Allah Almighty here showed that no one should be proud of their estimates and conjectures. Over every owner of knowledge is a possessor of knowledge, and His knowledge alone is the true one. In this, there is a subtle warning for the Quraish, to whom this narration is related—that today, in whichever circumstances they are seeing God's Messenger, they should not fall into any misunderstanding. The true knowledge of circumstances belongs only to Allah. He knows what is hidden in the veils of the future, how light will emerge from the current darknesses, and how paths will be prepared for Islam and the Prophet of Islam.

They said, "If he steals - a brother of his has stolen before." But Joseph kept it within himself and did not reveal it to them. He said, "You are worse in position, and Allah is most knowing of what you describe."

Another Mischief of the Brothers: When the brothers saw that Benjamin was caught, they immediately, to hide their embarrassment, leveled an accusation against Joseph. They said: if he has stolen, it is not a strange thing. Before this, a brother of his has also stolen. In this way, they tried to give the impression: he is not our real brother but a stepbrother, and this habit of theft he has brought from his mother's side. When Joseph heard this point of theirs, the natural effect it had to have was had, but he gulped it down because at this stage he did not want to express himself. He merely said in his heart: you are highly bad people, and what you describe, Allah knows it well.

They said, "O Al-Aziz, indeed he has a father [who is] an old man, quite elderly, so take one of us in his place. Indeed, we see you to be of those who do good."

Flattery of Joseph (peace be upon him): When they saw that the game was lost, they immediately resorted to flattery, and addressing Joseph with the official title of "Al-Aziz", which was reserved for the high officials of Egypt, they requested with great pleading: Your Honor! His father has become very old, so stop one of us in his place and release him; we consider you a highly good man, we hope you will not deprive us of your favor.

He said, "[I seek] refuge in Allah that we should take except him with whom we found our possession. Indeed, we would then be unjust."

Joseph's Response: Joseph (peace be upon him) replied: refuge in Allah from this—that we should catch anyone other than the one with whom our thing was found. If we did so, we would be highly unjust. Here, the care of Joseph (peace be upon him) should be noted, in that he does not say: we should catch anyone other than the one who has stolen our thing; rather, in cautious words, he says: "except him with whom we found our possession."

So when they had despaired of him, they secluded themselves in private consultation. The eldest of them said, "Do you not know that your father has taken upon you a solemn oath by Allah and [how] before you failed in Joseph? So I will never leave the land until my father permits me or Allah decides for me; and He is the best of judges."

The Consultation of the Brothers: After Joseph's aforementioned reply, they despaired of him—that he is not going to release him. Now, what should be done? To consider this question, all brothers sat apart from people for consultation. The eldest said: remember that your father has taken a covenant from you in the name of Allah, and before this, the omission you committed in the matter of Joseph is well-known; so I am not going to leave this place until my father permits me or Allah decides for me, and He is the best of judges.

There is a difference of opinion as to which brother is meant by "kabeer" (eldest/leader). Reuben, who was eldest in age, or Judah, who was eldest in intellect and opinion. In our view, the word "kabeer", just as in "rather, this largest (kabeeruhum) of them did it" is for the greatness of rank and position, similarly here also it is to show the greatness of rank and position. If mere greatness of age were to be expressed, "akbaruhum" would have been appropriate. For this reason, if Judah is meant by it, this is more likely. In the Torah, his name has come too. It appears that in his heart, there was some soft corner for Joseph (peace be upon him). Probably he was the one who, at the time when consultations to kill Joseph were happening, advised throwing him into some well on the route instead of killing him—that some passing caravan would extract him, and in this way they would be saved from the sin of his murder and also succeed in putting him away.

81–82. "Return to your father and say, 'O our father, indeed your son has stolen, and we did not testify except to what we knew, and we were not guardians of the unseen. And ask the city in which we were and the caravan in which we returned; and indeed, we are truthful.'"

In "And ask the city" (was'al-il-qaryah), the conjoined is omitted; i.e., "and ask the people of the city".

Judah gave this advice to his brothers: I am not leaving from here, but you people go to your father and say to him: your son has committed theft; what has come to our knowledge, we are saying only that; we are not knowers of the unseen; ask the people of the town where we stayed, and also ask the caravan with whom we returned, we are entirely truthful in our statement. The brothers acted upon this advice and in the end, gave the report of the event to their father. Now ahead is the mention of Jacob's (peace be upon him) reactions.

[Jacob] said, "Rather, your souls have enticed you to something, so patience is most fitting. Perhaps Allah will bring them to me all together. Indeed, He is the Knowing, the Wise."

"Tasweel" means decoration and facilitation. "Sawwala lahu-ash-shaytaan": Satan misguided him and put it in his eyes to perform some deed. "Sawwalat lahu nafsuhu kaza": his soul made some work easy for him and put it in his eyes. "Sabrun Jameel": the described indefinite noun here is in the place of the subject, and its predicate is omitted—namely, "beautiful patience alone is most fitting and worthy of adoption in this state." "Sabrun Jameel" is that patience which is clean of shallow ways of expressing grief. Everyone performs patience after wailing and mourning, but the patience of those who do good is that they bear the grief in such a way that neither is their tongue polluted with any word of complaint, nor does any action contrary to dignity issue from their hands and feet.

Jacob's (peace be upon him) Reactions on Being Deprived of Two Sons: The apparent meaning of "He said" (qaala) is indeed that Jacob spoke this addressing his sons, but since this word also comes for saying in the heart, for this reason, my mind turns again and again to the point that Jacob, hearing this grief-giving news from his sons, did not say this addressing them, but said this in his heart: this is all your self-fabricated story, so beautiful patience alone is most fitting to be adopted. At the same time, in his brilliant heart, this hope arose with great force: Allah Almighty will gather all of them to me, He alone is Knowing and Wise. Just as the intensity of heat increases the hope of rain, similarly the intensity of trial gives food and strength to the hope of Allah's mercy. The special servants of God are aware of this secret: Allah does not test His servants beyond their strength, which is why when they see that the matter is going beyond their strength, they become waiting that now the opening of the door is not far. When Jacob saw that after Joseph, he was deprived of Benjamin and Judah too, suddenly he saw this light: now, God willing, the time of everyone's gathering has come near. As for how this will happen, the answer to this "how" is only with Him Who is the Master of all knowledge and wisdom. The servant should keep reliance only on this Knowing and Wise.

And he turned away from them and said, "Oh, my sorrow over Joseph," and his eyes became white from grief, for he was [suppressing] a grim [sorrow].

"And he turned away from them"—that is, after this calamity, being distressed by his sons, he went and sat in a corner. This fresh calamity renewed Joseph's (peace be upon him) grief with further intensity, and in this intensity of grief, "Oh, my sorrow over Joseph" came out of his tongue on its own. Due to the intensity of grief and frequency of weeping, his eyes became white, and due to suppressing the grief in his chest at all times, he remained suffocated. It should remain clear that all these states of his are being described regarding his private grief and sorrow, in which the matter was entirely between him and his Lord. He also expressed this reality: "I only complain of my suffering and my grief to Allah, and I know from Allah that which you do not know." Expressing one's grief and sorrow before one's Lord is not only not a bad thing, but rather highly good. The cause of this grief and sorrow is not despair, but hope. The servant expects: the more he weeps before his Lord, the more his weeping will bring His mercy into surge. That weeping and crying is prohibited which is before others, which is the manifestation of despair instead of hope, and in which man expresses his lack of patience in such a way that the dignity of faith and reliance is injured.

It is not a strange thing that this fresh calamity also revived only Joseph's grief in Jacob's heart. Firstly, the nature of Benjamin's calamity was not that which Joseph's calamity was; secondly, the attachment Jacob had with Joseph was not merely due to being his son, but in it the primary factor was his high capabilities on the basis of which Jacob loved him to such a degree that he became the object of his brothers' jealousy, and eventually as a result of it, he had to pass through highly severe trials at the hands of his brothers.

The whiteness of the eyes due to the intensity of grief and sorrow and weeping is not an exaggerated style of expression, but the expression of reality. From grief and weeping, the blackness of the eyelashes and pupils is also affected, and the red veins of the eyes also gradually disappear. The word "kazeem" is for praising Jacob's patience—that due to suppressing the grief in his chest at all times, he remained suffocated. In "ya asafa", the alif at the end stands in place of the pronoun of possessiveness (ya of idafah).

They said, "By Allah, you will not cease remembering Joseph until you become critically ill or become of those who perish."

"By Allah" (Ta-Allah)—in Arabic, like "wa", "ta" also comes for swearing. "Tafta'u" is in the meaning of "la tazaalu" (you will not cease). "Harad" refers to that person who reaches near destruction and becomes wasted.

The Sons' Scolding to Their Father: The sons, after performing all this, started advising their father: by God! You will keep remembering Joseph in this way until you become wasted or perish. It should be kept in mind: the style of this advice, although apparently sympathetic, secretly has that same emotion of jealousy working within it which was the cause of all their actions mentioned above. They had put away Joseph in the expectation that after this, their father's entire attention would turn towards them; but now they saw that the result of their this failed attempt emerged entirely contrary. If any corner of their father's attention and regard belonged to them before, now they were deprived of that too. They saw that their father had no other work than to sit in the corner of solitude, remember Joseph, weep, and seek help and cry before his Lord.

He said, "I only complain of my suffering and my grief to Allah, and I know from Allah that which you do not know."

The exact translation of "bathth" is anxiety.

The Father's Response: Jacob said in reply: do not scold me for this grief and sorrow of mine, I complain of my anxiety and my grief only to Allah, not to anyone else. That weeping and crying is bad which is before anyone other than God; if a servant weeps only before his Lord, this is no evil, rather this too is worship. And do not think that I am performing some useless work. In God's matter, your experience is one thing, my experience another. I know those things from God with which you are not acquainted; so leave me to my state, I am not hopeless of my Lord's mercy.

"O my sons, go and find out about Joseph and his brother and despair not of relief from Allah. Indeed, no one despairs of relief from Allah except the disbelieving people."

When the matter reached the point that Jacob opened this secret of his heart before his sons—that he is not hopeless of his Lord's mercy in the matter of Joseph—in plain words, in a highly loving manner, he also said to them: O my sons, go and find out about Joseph and his brother, and do not despair of God's relief and His support and mercy; only disbelievers despair of Allah's support and mercy. The meaning: if you perform this work with the struggle of body and soul, a single gust of divine mercy will make all difficulties easy.

So when they entered upon Joseph, they said, "O Al-Aziz, adversity has touched us and our family, and we have come with goods of poor quality, so give us full measure and be charitable to us. Indeed, Allah rewards the charitable."

"Durr" refers to that adversity which befalls due to hunger and famine etc. "Bida'atun muzjaat": such merchandise which no one would accept; contemptible, unwanted. From the use of this word, the mind turns to the point that to pay the price, instead of cash, they had brought some such commodity which was not in demand.

Appearance before Joseph again: It is not estimated from here what effect their father's appeal had on them, but the requirement of grain compelled them to go to Egypt again. There, when they appeared before Joseph, they described their miserable state and anxiety in a highly moving manner. Addressing Joseph with his official title of "Al-Aziz", they said: Your Honor! We and our family are in severe adversity due to the famine, we have brought highly poor merchandise this time, but our request is: give us full grain, and moreover, bless us with charity too; Allah Almighty rewards those who give charity.

He said, "Do you know what you did with Joseph and his brother when you were ignorant?"

Revealing the Secret: Joseph (peace be upon him) was a brother, and an honorable brother. This miserable state and anxiety of his brothers, and their this pleading request, especially the request for charity, shook his heart. They were people of great pride, but now circumstances had so loosened their nerves that they had no shame even in requesting charity. Joseph was so influenced by this situation that he did not consider it appropriate to maintain the veil of estrangement which was between him and his brothers. It was said: do you remember what you did with Joseph and his brother in the era of your ignorance? The word "jahl" (ignorance) here is used in the meaning of being overcome by bad emotions. Its explanation has passed under multiple places.

They said, "Are you indeed Joseph?" He said, "I am Joseph, and this is my brother. Allah has certainly been gracious to us. Indeed, he who fears Allah and is patient, then indeed, Allah does not allow to be lost the reward of those who do good."

As soon as the word "Joseph" came out of Joseph's tongue, these people spoke out: oh! Are you Joseph? From their this sentence, this point is expressed: in the previous meetings too, from his face, appearance, style, and manners, they had had this doubt: could he be Joseph? But between that well of Canaan, in which they had thrown Joseph, and this throne of the kingdom, on which Joseph was menez now, there was such a distance that they could not assume that Joseph could reach here from there. Those whose gaze remains limited only to the apparent cannot conceive of these wonders of divine power and mercy. Accordingly, whenever their mind went towards the point that many of his glimpses are of Joseph, they dismissed it considering it a mere play of illusion; but now, when the aforementioned sentence of Joseph fell in their ears, they cried out: oh, are you Joseph? Joseph said: yes! I am Joseph, and this is my brother. Allah has been gracious to us. Those who adopt piety and remain steadfast on it with beauty, Allah does not allow to be lost the reward of such doers of good (muhsineen); rather, He will bless them with His favor in this world too, and in the Hereafter too He will grant them full reward.

This very reality is the soul of this entire narration, and we have submitted in the beginning that this very thing holds the status of the central theme of this Surah. The word "ihsan" (doing good) is mentioned here as the fundamental condition of piety and patience—that is, piety and patience accepted with Allah are those within which lies the soul of "ihsan", meaning the servant performs the right of piety and patience in such a way as if he is seeing God, and believes that if he is not seeing God, God is beholding him.

They said, "By Allah, certainly has Allah preferred you over us, and indeed, we have been sinners."

"Ithaar" means preferring and granting excellence.

Admission of the Truth: At this stage, they swore and admitted: undoubtedly, God has preferred you over us, and we were mistaken. That is, we did all this in our anger: why our father looks upon you with special love and affection. Now Allah Almighty Himself has granted you clear excellence over us, and made our mistake clear to us. We accept that you deserved the excellence you achieved, and what we did, we were mistaken in it.

He said, "No blame will be upon you today. May Allah forgive you; and He is the most merciful of the merciful."

"Tathreeb" means scolding someone for his blameworthy action.

Joseph's (peace be upon him) Forgiveness and Kindness: If any person admits his mistake with a true feeling of embarrassment, an honorable son of the honorable forgives him, and with Allah Almighty too there is hope of his forgiveness, because He is the most merciful of the merciful. When Joseph felt that these people were admitting their mistake with a sincere heart, he forgave them with great generosity: now there is no blame upon you; and also made them hope for forgiveness with God.

Remember this point here: these exact words were spoken by the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) on the day of the conquest of Mecca addressing those chiefs of the Quraish who had been active in his enmity. When these people appeared before the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), he asked them: do you know what dealing I am going to make with you? They said: you are an honorable brother and the son of an honorable brother. He said: I say to you the same point which my brother Joseph said to his brothers; today there is no blame upon you, go, you are free.

"Take this, my shirt, and cast it over the face of my father; he will become seeing. And bring me your family all together."

The Miracles of Joseph's Shirt: The single cure for that weakness of sight whose cause is grief, sorrow, and weeping, as passed in verse 84, is that some such thing comes before the eyes which not only washes away all this grief and sorrow, but the joy it brings runs a new life in every vein. Through Benjamin, Joseph had come to know of his father's state: his eyes had become white due to weeping. Accordingly, he gave his shirt to his brothers: take this and cast it over my father's face; from this, his eyes will become bright. Remember here: the greatest grief of life had also come before Jacob in the form of Joseph's shirt when the brothers presented it with blood before their old father: a wolf ate Joseph; and now the greatest joy of life was also to manifest in the form of Joseph's shirt.

Why did Joseph send only the shirt touched with his body as his sign? And from where did this effect come in the shirt that sight returned from it? These questions are neither for everyone to solve, nor can their solution enter everyone's understanding. These things have connection with emotions, and emotions of such highly elevated people: on one side is Jacob, on the other Joseph. We, the commoners, in such matters, cannot say anything more than this: the wonders of the effect of true emotions manifest in such amazing forms that reason remains completely unable to explain them. "And bring me your family all together"—Joseph also directed his brothers: come here all together with your family. The mention of the parents is omitted due to extreme clarity. Obviously, the primary objective was to call them. All the rest held the status of their dependents.

And when the caravan departed [from Egypt], their father said, "Indeed, I find the smell of Joseph, if you did not think me senile."

"Fasala, fasoolan" means departing and coming out of some place, and "tafneed" means considering someone senile and foolish and declaring his opinion and word to be weightless and wrong.

The Smell of Joseph's Shirt: As soon as the caravan departed from Egypt, Jacob said to those around him: if you people do not declare me senile, I would say that I find the smell of Joseph. Extreme focus, even in normal conditions, increases the power of man's senses much. Here, the matter is also of a special nature, and the power of senses is also of a Prophet like Jacob. For this reason, there should be no wonder at the point that Jacob found the smell of Joseph's shirt from so far. We should not compare the powers and senses of the Prophets with our own. With them, Allah Almighty's dealing is special.

"The philosopher indeed is denier of the Hannanah, He is alien to the senses of the Prophets."

They said, "By Allah, indeed you are in your old delusion."

Upon this, those listening said the same of which Jacob had expressed apprehension. They said: you are still in your same old delusion in which you were before.

And when the bearer of good tidings arrived, he cast it over his face, and he returned seeing. He said, "Did I not tell you that I know from Allah that which you do not know?"

In "And when... arrived" (Falamma an ja'a), before "an" is an omitted verb. That is: this event occurred that the bearer of glad tidings arrived, so he cast Joseph's shirt over Jacob's face, and his eyes became bright. He said: "Did I not tell you that I know from Allah that which you do not know?" This is the reference to that point which has passed behind in verse 86. When the point he had spoken manifested in the form of the event, he expressed joy on it, and also taught his sons: they should not live their lives merely as slaves of the apparent, but within this apparent, they should also believe in the workings of God's hidden hands. Without this, the right of faith and reliance upon God cannot be performed.

They said, "O our father, ask for us forgiveness of our sins; indeed, we have been sinners."

Requesting Forgiveness from the Father: After seeing all this, the sons realized their foolishness, and just as they admitted their mistake before Joseph, the mention of which has passed in verse 91, similarly they admitted their mistake before their father, and requested him: pray to Allah Almighty for the forgiveness of our sins.

He said, "I will ask forgiveness for you from my Lord. Indeed, He is the Forgiving, the Merciful."

Jacob accepted their this petition, and promised them: soon I will pray to my Lord for the forgiveness of your sins. The word "sawfa" (soon) here is pointing to the point that Jacob promised to pray for them with special preparation, at a special time. He did not dismiss them by saying: go, may Allah forgive you. From this, one point was known: if a person's heart is embarrassed of his sins, he should also request the righteous for prayer, and in his favor, the prayer of the righteous is accepted. The second point was known: thus, a servant can pray to his Lord at any time, but special preparation, special time, and special place are also effective matters in the acceptance of prayer.

"Every speech has its place, and every point its station."

So when they entered upon Joseph, he took his parents to himself and said, "Enter Egypt, if Allah wills, safe [and secure]."

In the Presence of Joseph (peace be upon him): Here, due to the presence of the context, so much is omitted: complying with Joseph's instruction, these people arrived in Egypt along with their parents and family. Arriving in Egypt, when they appeared before Joseph, he gave his parents place near him, and welcomed all of them. "Enter Egypt, if Allah wills, safe [and secure]" is the sentence of welcome—that is, enter Egypt, this entry will be, if Allah wills, of safety and peace. It is known from the Torah that Joseph came out of the city to welcome them, and brought them into the city in such a style that a celebration was formed.

The word "abawayhi" (his parents) here is used predominantly, because it is known from the Torah that Joseph's mother had passed away. He was brought up by his stepmother, who was also his maternal aunt.

And he raised his parents upon the throne, and they fell down before him prostrate. And he said, "O my father, this is the explanation of my vision of before; my Lord has made it reality. And He was certainly good to me when He took me out of prison and brought you [here] from bedouin life after Satan had induced [estrangement] between me and my brothers. Indeed, my Lord is Subtle in what He wills. Indeed, He is the Knowing, the Wise."

By "arsh" (throne) here is not meant the royal throne, but rather that seat on which Joseph (peace be upon him) sat to decide cases etc. It appears that in that era, ministers and high officials sat on seats. "Kharra, yakhirru" means falling down from above, and "sujood" means bowing obediently. Placing the forehead on the ground is not necessary for it, but rather this is its completed form; when the word "kharra" comes with it, its meaning is falling down involuntarily. Regardless of whether this falling down is only to the extent of bowing, or to the extent of placing the forehead on the ground. The era of this event, from the study of the Torah etc., it is known that in that time, this method was well-known for showing respect to elders, but its form was not placing the forehead on the ground, but bowing. And if any person interprets this sujood as the technical prostration, even then no special difference is made, because prostration is not shirk in itself, but rather among the similarities and forms of shirk, and these similarities and forms were strictly prohibited in the final and complete religion—namely, Islam.

Joseph (peace be upon him) gave his parents place on the seat out of respect. The remaining people must have sat on the place of common people according to the custom. After this, it appears that Joseph appeared, and according to the custom, when his retinue bowed for his respect, being influenced by the environment, these people also involuntarily bowed for his respect.

The Interpretation of the Dream: "And he said, 'O my father, this is the explanation of my vision of before; my Lord has made it reality.'" In "ya abati", "ta" stands in place of "ya" of possessiveness. When Joseph saw this scene, he remembered his that dream which he had seen before—that eleven stars and the sun and the moon are prostrating to me. Addressing his father, he said: O father! This is the interpretation of my dream which I saw before; my Lord has made it true. After this, Joseph expressed gratitude to his Lord on his release from the prison and the gathering of his parents and brothers after a period of separation. "Indeed, my Lord is Subtle in what He wills"—that is, my Lord, for the work He wants to perform, extracts such subtle paths by His knowledge and His wisdom that even an estimate of them does not occur. This entire narration of Joseph (peace be upon him) is a grand testimony to this reality.

"My Lord, You have given me [something] of sovereignty and taught me of the interpretation of dreams. Creator of the heavens and the earth, You are my protector in this world and the Hereafter. Cause me to die a Muslim and join me with the righteous."

Seeing this scene, Joseph's (peace be upon him) heart drowns in the praise and gratitude of God. He expresses gratitude to his Lord: sovereignty too You alone have granted me, and the knowledge of the interpretation of dreams too You alone have blessed; O Creator of the heavens and the earth, in both the world and the Hereafter, my Helper and Protector is You alone. Grant me death upon Islam, and include me in the group of the righteous.

The Subject Ahead. Verses 102 to 111: Concluding Verses of the Surah: Now these are the concluding verses of the Surah, in which the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is addressed to console you, and at the same time, the Quraish are threatened: if they do not learn a lesson from the history of nations, they must be prepared to face that end which past nations have faced. Recite the verses.

"That is from the news of the unseen which We reveal, [O Muhammad], to you. And you were not with them when they put together their plan while they were conspiring."

"That" (dhalika) is pointing to this narration which was related. It is said: this is of the news of the Unseen—that is, this is a thing completely outside your knowledge and information, neither did you possess any knowledge of it, nor did you possess any medium to become aware of it. This is merely Allah's favor that He informed you of it through His revelation; and you were not present with Joseph's brothers when they were conspiring against Joseph—one's opinion was one thing, another's another, and eventually all agreed on this opinion that he should be thrown into some well.

It should be kept in mind here: although Joseph's story is indeed present in the Torah, for the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) there was no medium to become aware of it because you were unlettered. Then, at every step, there is a difference between the description of the Torah and the description of the Quran, and whoever ponders over all these differences cannot help but accept that the description of the Quran is entirely according to reason and nature, because it is based directly on divine revelation.

And most of the people, although you should desire it, are not believers.

The Real Cause of the Deniers' Turning Away: That is: if in the temperaments there is the desire and capability to accept the truth, this one thing is sufficient to prove the Quran as divine revelation. For the Jews and Christians too there is proof in this, and for the Quraish too; but the capability to accept the truth has not remained in the hearts of these people. For this reason, no matter what you do in the desire for their faith and guidance, their majority will remain deprived of faith. The cause of their not believing is not that the Quran's being a divine Book is not clear to them; rather, its real cause is that the capability to accept the truth has died within them.

And you do not ask them for it any reward. It is not but a reminder to the worlds.

Consolation to the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon whom): This is consolation for the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): if these people do not believe, why after all do you keep yourself so anxious behind them? You were distributing this blessing free of cost, you were not demanding of them any reward for it—such that if they did not accept it, there would be some decrease in your reward. The deprivation is theirs, not yours. This Quran is only a reminder for the creation; whoever accepts it, its benefit is for himself; whoever does not accept it, its loss he alone will suffer; your responsibility is only to convey it to people. After that, you are exonerated.

And how many a sign within the heavens and earth do they pass by with faces turned away?

Response to the Demand for Signs: That is: in their not believing, this thing also has no share—that they are not shown any sign according to their demand. The actual reality is: the eyes to see the signs and obtain lesson from them are not possessed by them; otherwise, how many signs are there in the heavens and the earth which they pass by; if they possessed eyes, they would see them and learn lessons from them. The meaning is: from this perspective too, neither is the fault yours, nor the Quran's; rather, the real disease is within themselves—that they are not prepared to open their eyes to see anything. It should be kept in mind: when the Quran gave them this news—that if they did not believe in the Messenger, Allah's punishment would come upon them—their reply was: why is some sign of this punishment not shown to us? This is its reply—that if they open their eyes and see, there are many signs around them, but they consider only that to be a sign which falls upon them.

And most of them do not believe in Allah except while they associate partners with Him.

Another Cause of Not Believing: This is another major cause clarified for their not believing in the Quran: among them, those who believe in Allah also believe in many partners and intercessors along with Allah, and their belief is that if Allah wants to catch them for any matter, these partners and intercessors will save them from God. In the presence of this misguidance, how can the warnings of the Quran work on them?

Then do they feel secure that there will not come to them an overwhelming punishment from Allah or that the Hour will not come upon them suddenly while they do not perceive?

This question is for warning and alerting—that: relying on their partners and intercessors, have they become so unconcerned as to think that no such calamity of divine punishment will come upon them which will overwhelm all of them, or that the Hour will not suddenly arrive, and they will have no news of it? The meaning is: if this thing is there, then indeed their destruction has come.

Say, "This is my way; I invite to Allah with insight, I and those who follow me. And exalted is Allah; and I am not of those who associate partners with Him."

Announcement of Exoneration from Shirk: The root of all their diseases was this shirk, which is why when its mention came, the announcement of exoneration from it was made through the Prophet's tongue with full clarity. It was said: tell them, my path is this. I invite to Allah, with complete insight, I and those who follow me—that is, just as I invite to Allah alone, similarly my followers also invite to Allah alone, and we all carry with us the complete light of argument and insight in this matter. The meaning: in this matter, let no one expect of us that we will be ready to show any kind of softness, or tolerate any confusion. Allah is clean and sanctified of all pollutions of shirk. And I am not among the polytheists—meaning I announce my exoneration from them.

And We sent not before you, [O Muhammad], except men to whom We inspired from among the people of the cities. Have they not traveled through the land and observed how was the end of those before them? And the home of the Hereafter is better for those who fear Allah; then will you not use reason?

Objection to Appointing a Human as Messenger: This refutes another objection of theirs, which they used to present as an excuse for not believing. Their objection was: how is it possible that we should accept a human from among us, who was born among us, lived among us, and grew up among us, as Allah's Messenger? If Allah had to send some Messenger, He would have sent some superior creature, namely an angel, as a Messenger. Addressing the Messenger of Allah (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), the reply was given: before you too, the Messengers We sent, without exception, We sent all of them from among humans alone, We did not send them from among angels or jinn. If they possessed any distinction, it was not that they were some superior, unique, or alien creature, but that they were honored with God's revelation. "Have they not traveled through the land..."—that is, if they had traveled in their own land with open eyes, they would have also found the proof of the aforementioned reality, and from the ruins of 'Aad, Thamud, Madyan etc., this point would have also become clear to them: what was the end of those who denied these Messengers, and those who believed in them, what reward did they receive? And the real place of reward for the believers is the Hereafter, which is far greater than this, so do you not think.

[They were reprieved] until, when the messengers despaired and were certain that they had been denied, there came to them Our victory, and whoever We willed was saved. And Our punishment cannot be repelled from the people who are criminals.

The Divine Sunnah regarding Divine Punishment: Now, in reply to the haste of the disbelievers—that if the Prophet warns us of punishment, why does this punishment not come—Allah Almighty has clarified His sunnah in this matter: Allah does not make haste in sending punishment, but rather He completes His proof upon people through His Messengers. Punishment comes when Allah's Messengers despair of their nations' faith, and the people of their nation, due to the delay of the punishment, begin to assume that they were falsely threatened with the punishment. It is said: at that time, Our help manifests for Our Messengers, so whom We will receives safety, and from the criminals, no power can repel Our punishment.

There was certainly in their stories a lesson for those of understanding. It is not a invented narrative, but a confirmation of what was before it and a detailed explanation of all things and a guidance and mercy for a people who believe.

The Real Nature of the Quran: That is: within the stories of the past Prophets and their nations lies great lessons for those of understanding, provided they use intellect and do not listen to these narrations merely as stories of others, but rather obtain lessons from them to correct their own lives. This Quran is no fabricated story, but rather, of the predictions and realities present in the heavenly Books from before, it is their confirmation, detailed explanation of every relevant thing, guidance from the perspective of the beginning, and mercy from the perspective of the end, for those who believe.

These are the final lines which this humble servant was granted the ability to write in the commentary of this Surah.

And our final call is that all praise belongs to Allah, the Lord of the worlds.
